text: John Cazale’s five films. I think technically it’s six years with the release date of The Deer Hunter, but that’s quibbling. All his films were best picture nominees, and many of his peers considered him one of the finest actors, before he tragically died from cancer. Ok, commenting AGAIN because of the length auto mod deleting me. Cazale did The Godfather (1972), and The Godfather Part II (1974). He also appeared in Coppola's The Conversation (1974) and Sidney Lumet's Dog Day Afternoon (1975), the latter of which earned him a Golden Globe Award nomination. In 1977, Cazale was diagnosed with lung cancer, but chose to complete his role in The Deer Hunter(1978). Shortly after filming was completed, he died in New York City on March 13, 1978, aged 42. For only really starring in five films, that’s just batting a thousand. He’s so astonishing in each role, too. And he was never even nominated for an Oscar. Pacino said of him: “John Cazale, in general, was one of the great actors of our time — that time, any time. I learned so much from him. I had done a lot of theater and three films with him. He was inspiring, he just was. And he didn't get credit for any of it. He was in five films, all Oscar-nominated films, and he was great in all of them. He was particularly great in Godfather II and I don't think he got that kind of recognition.”
label: analysis
notes: Named films + peer testimony as evidence for Cazale claim; pre-labeled

text: The shark effect I assure you in its time was freaking terrifying. (Yes, I saw the film way, waaaaay back in the Stone Age when it was originally released... cough-old-fart-cough). Back then, unlike now, there was no instant access to real shark footage. For many of us, this was the very first time we saw a shark presented in such a way and... it really worked. What I've always been fascinated about regarding this movie is the way it tightens the suspense slowly but surely. We have 3 leads: The veteran shark hunter (Robert Shaw) whose whole life seems to be about capturing sharks. We, as the audience, feel confident he will surely capture the beast. We have the book-smart shark expert (Richard Dreyfuss). If Robert Shaw's Quint doesn't capture the beast, surely Dreyfuss' Hooper will figure out a way, using the latest technology, to capture and kill it. And then there's Roy Scheider's Sheriff Brody. Presented in the story as a literal fish out of water (ha!), he is uncomfortable in his job here while determined to do what's right. By the end of the story, he states, as if we didn't know, how he used to hate the water...! If there's a character who we think has the least experience and chance of getting the beast, it's him. So what the film does so freaking wonderfully is strip us first of the veteran shark hunter. Mentally, he's defeated when he tries to run back to shore, fleeing the shark while blowing the Orca's motor. A little bit later, he would be defeated a second time and gone. In between, Hooper tries his skills and is also defeated (and, for a little while, we fear he's dead as well). Suddenly, the two people audiences felt would surely defeat the beast are gone and we're left with... the city guy. The one who doesn't know a damn thing about sharks. Who doesn't like the water. Who has to read up on sharks in his dining room to figure out what they're about. It's a great bit of subtle storytelling which raises the tension so wonderfully for audiences as we enter the movie's climactic third act!
label: reaction
notes: Personal first-viewing account; "terrifying" is experiential not argumentative; historical context colors the feeling

text: Now that's a wild philosophical take on the story... and I love it! ...however... I do still believe the more surface level view is what they were going for mostly: The idea that the audience feels this monster cannot be brought down but in Quint and Hooper we have two potential champions: The veteran monster hunter/killer who is brought down not once but twice (mentally, then physically) and the young tech-savy shark expert whose state of the art weapons surely will take the monster down if the veteran cannot... ...and then, they both fail and we're left with the guy who seems weakest to do the job being forced to do just that! By the way, Spielberg and company sorta tried to repeat this in Jurassic Park with the Robert Muldoon character. He was supposed to be this big experienced hunter who was supposed to know how to handle the dinosaurs and then, he's outwitted and killed by them early on. However, unlike Jaws, we had too many characters running around Jurassic Park and this notching up the level of suspense wasn't quite replicated in his death versus Quint's. At least IMHO!
label: analysis
notes: Argues surface reading (Quint/Hooper as champions) over philosophical one; uses specific character roles as evidence

text: What's even more amazing about all the story telling with the camera you are mentioning, is that the this was accomplished while out on a REAL OCEAN. Most people don't understand how difficult that is and how completely unprecedented it was then and even now. The only movie to come close to this is Waterworld, and it 100 times the budget. There is a good reason why nobody shoots 100% on a real ocean. It's because it sucks, it's VERY difficult. Between this and Apocalypse Now, to me there are no other films that feel so real, because they WERE real. In both movies, there are virtually no "effects" (you can't even call the shark an effect because it was mechanical and technically "real" from a film perspective). I also agree with how mesmerizing Shaw is in this role, the best performance of his life, and probably the best cast character of all time, I can't really think of a better one. This movie, along with Star Wars are what I call perfect movies in terms of story telling. In a good story, you don't even notice the time passing, everything just flows so fluidly between acts and scenes, it has the perfect balance of plot development, action and "dead space" so you don't get overwhelmed.
label: analysis
notes: Argues real-ocean filming is unprecedented; names a specific comparator film; evidence is verifiable

text: There are two ways people use the term "blockbuster", one is based purely on metrics. So a movie that makes a ton of money. The second, which I think is the more common one now, is a movie made by a major studio with the specific intention to make a lot of money.  A lot of people have problems with the second because it's more commerce driven that art driven.
label: analysis
notes: Defines "blockbuster" two ways and uses the distinction to explain critical discourse; definitional argument with mechanism

text: Blockbuster films are already very popular by virtue of their definition. In order to be globally successful, any ideas that might be challenging or offensive to conservative markets or unsophisticated or uneducated audience members, including children, need to be removed or downplayed. Products made with this intention are usually quite generic, and offer little substantive value other than lowest common denominator entertainment. McDonalds is probably the best selling burger in the world. I don't believe their bottom line is affected by what people have to say about it, and I doubt its reputation needs defending.
label: analysis
notes: Causal chain: global success → remove challenging content → artistic compromise; specific logic, not just assertion

text: Nolan's visual language has always been consistent, and it's always leaned heavily on the cinematographer he's working with. His work with Wally Pfister (everything up till The Dark Knight Rises) basically looked similar to one another: similar lighting (Pfister is a pretty unintrusive cinematographer who favors minimal but punchy sourcing, but doesn't call a ton of attention to himself), similar lens textures and palette, etc. He switched to Hoyte van Hoytema with Interstellar after Pfister made a go at directing, and his style is distinct enough (especially in his lighting) that there was a noticeable shift in Nolan's visual language at that time. Villeneuve, by contrast, swaps cinematographers constantly: despite his career being much shorter, he's worked with André Turpin, Pierre Gill, Nicolas Bolduc, Roger Deakins , Bradford Young, Greig Fraser, and Linus Sandgren. What's more, he clearly gives them a fair amount of leeway given how much each of the films shot by those cinematographers resembles the other films shot by each of them with different directors. Nolan's not really an outlier, and neither is Denis: there are plenty of directors who stick to one DP and plenty who don't, and additionally there are director/DP combos (Eggers/Blaschke is a good example) who self-consciously switch things up film-to-film, but the idea of a director/DOP honing a consistent visual language is by no means unusual. One could say the same for Spielberg/Kaminski, to name one: everything they've done, particularly in the DI era, tends to look very consistent accross multiple films. Now, if the question was whether the Nolan/Hoytema house style was sufficiently interesting, I'm liable to be more critical, ha.
label: analysis
notes: Names Pfister and van Hoytema by name; contrasts their styles with specific films; argument is checkable

text: I agree with the majority of your point, but it’s interesting that you bring up Villeneuve as an example of the opposite. What you said about being disappointed by The Odyssey’s apparent lack of “something strange, dreamlike…or spiritually uncanny” is exactly my problem with his approach to the Dune movies. They’re extremely competently made, and add a much-needed dose of seriousness to science fiction in a landscape that has been marred by Marvel-like snark and winks to the audience. But there’s absolutely none of the bizarreness or psychedelia of Herbert’s original vision. It’s this very safe, straightforward, monochromatic rendering of his world that strikes me as a tad boring and unimaginative. The dream/prophecy sequences, which should’ve been an opportunity to untether from the stoic realism of the rest of the films, end up looking like perfume commercials to me. There’s a slick minimalism in his Dune films that verges on brutalism, to the point that there’s no real stylistic distinction between the Harkonnen and Atreides armies. It’s telling that the most visually distinctive moment in both films is the switch to black-and-white for Feyd’s fight in the gladiator arena. I get that Villenueve is being intentional in stripping down his sci-fi to stand apart from the goofiness and poppy colors of something like Guardians of the Galaxy, but when you see Lynch’s Dune or the concept art for Jodorowsky’s planned adaptation, it just strikes me as dull and overly sterile by comparison. That’s why, as flawed as Lynch’s Dune is, I still find it to be the more interesting and imaginative adaptation. The choices he made regarding makeup, costumes, production design, special effects, etc. feel distinctive in a way that Villenueve’s films just aren’t. I’d be curious to know why The Odyssey’s visual palette strikes you as being boring or samey but Dune’s doesn’t.
label: analysis
notes: Uses Villeneuve/Dune as counter-example; references specific quality ("dreamlike, spiritually uncanny") as missing

text: I see what you mean. They all feel like they were made by someone who really enjoyed the cinematography of Interstellar. I think it works fine, as they share a specific "cinematic" look which lends to the epic feeling of the grand narratives you see play out across the films you've mentioned. But it does feel a little uninspired. Interstellar was a film about human extinction, perseverance, and some of the realest and most raw feelings of the human condition. It really earns that sense of scale. The others sort of chase it in a way.

text: He's always seemed like a director who's focused more on the technical aspects of film. That's not a bad thing. I'd put James Cameron, George Lucas, and Robert Zemeckis in that category too. Decent storytellers who are looking for different tools to incorporate into their process. But it does feel more and more like Nolan has rested on his laurels in favor of spectacle. Not sure what that is, other than maybe he found a formula that works for him and applies it to everything he's done for the last 10 years. I would love to see him take more chances (not just by working with bigger budgets) because he's still one of a handful of directors that can pull an audience in on name recognition alone.

text: Nolan only really has one genre, making another utterly mediocre mid-concept entertainment about any given great human universal. He's done memory, dreams, time, space, nuclear weapons, crime, trauma, illusion … now he's doing Homeric myth. Nolan forcibly misunderstands the feelings, people and conceptual stakes of every subject matter with which his pseud talent engages. This guy can film Oppenheimer and Tatlock sitting nude opposite each other without the merest frisson of eroticism. World is ending due to climate change? Humanity must be in need of some bullshit corny Oedipal redemption followed by the image of a new frontier of American settlement, settler wagons on a new planetary surface under a proud stars and stripes. Dreams? You can go inside other people's and they look like level designs from Quake fan mods. Batman? He's a rich fascist, but that's actually a good thing. Memory? Yes, if you redact enough, original sin turns out to be real. Matter and energy can pass through time from a dystopian future to about 2015? Well let's send some visored combat goons with bullets that shoot in reverse and definitely not any stock market bets. THE PRESTIGE is Nolan's only properly good film, and I reckon it's because it's the one with an adapted screenplay from a brilliant Chris Priest novel. If Nolan can get Matt Damon to play "the man with twists and turns" and land it I'll be absolutely gobsmacked.
label: hot_take
notes: "Only really has one genre" — bold categorical claim; topics listed (memory, dreams, space) used rhetorically, not as evidence

text: It's a tricky film to draw a neat circle around. I think jealousy and status are pretty intertwined as themes, with a heavy emphasis on women being a commodity that is emblematic of ones status. I always saw his relationship with Alice as more of a functional one for the purposes of elevating and maintaining status, whereas he develops a more genuine connection with the overdosing woman or even the prostitute. Moreover, Cruise's character is profoundly jealous of the idea that his wife has shared a genuine connection with that other man (I think he was a pilot or something). I can't summarize it neatly, but there's always been a strong connection between those ideas and the purpose of the cult. Everyone encountered in the movie seems to have hidden, unfulfilled sexual urges (Cruise and his wife, the costume shop owner and his "customers", the host of the party at the beginning etc.), and Cruise's character explores that rabbit hole as it gets more and more depraved. Ultimately, ending on the idea that the depravity is so ubiquitous they may have already infected his home. It goes from "rich guy cheats who cares" to "holy shit this is a cabal". But it's been a while since I've seen it, but I love that movie to pieces. The "sir your taxi cab is waiting" scene floored me the first time I saw it.
label: analysis
notes: Thematic argument: jealousy/status intertwined; reads Bill's relationship with Alice as functional/instrumental; specific character claim

text: Two intertwined narratives. One man's sexual exploration, triggered by his wife's infidelity. At the beginning, he's this nice guy who doesn't even seem to understand there's something sexual in seeing his patients naked in his office. So he goes and this sexual discovery adventure where he discovers all the darker aspects of sex - prostitution, AIDS... And in exploring his own fantasies, he realises everyone is basically fucking his wife. That's a detail I don't see many people commenting on, but at the party every woman is a tall, slim blonde like Nicole Kidman. So his fantasies include his wife, which is kinda wholesome, and by the end of the movie this plot line is solved. They just have to "fuck". Now there's the other narrative, which is not as wholesome. The sum of every man's sexuality creates a world of sexual exploitation. The orgy party is a dream and it's also not a dream; it happens behind closed doors, but this violence is everywhere, all over the city, if you open your eyes to it: prostitution, exploitation, homophobia, rape and so on... At the end, I see the mask as a symbol of this duality in every man: capable of love and fidelity, but deep down, a potentially dangerous animal.
label: analysis
notes: Structural breakdown of EWS: two intertwined narratives, sexual discovery arc; causal chain from wife's infidelity

text: I'm sure there was a lot of kneejerk pearl clutching criticism of the nudity, but it's also very explicitly a film about the male gaze so I found the discourse around the extent to which it succeeds in critiquing the male gaze vs. embodying it (I think it does both simultaneously) quite interesting. I don't think you can really meaningfully talk about this film without talking about the sex scenes. A central theme is the difference in how the female character's sexuality operates when being observed by male characters vs. when they're unobserved, but there's an argument that in depicting their private sexual encounters explicitly, the voyeur is actually just shifted from within the narrative to behind the camera. I watched a pretty good video essay that contrasted this with the treatment of the same issue in Portrait of a Lady on Fire, which is also centrally concerned with "the gaze"/being observed but where the camera conspicuously cuts away just before the start of each sexual encounter to leave its protagonists genuinely unobserved.
label: analysis
notes: Argues nudity criticism misreads a film explicitly about the male gaze; "does both simultaneously" is a specific interpretive claim

text: It’s not just one of PCW’s finest, it’s one of the finest pieces of cinema of the 21st century. Aside from the production and craft noted by OP there are many other aspects of its making that really elevate it. The adaptation - Sarah Waters’s “Fingersmith”, upon which it is based, takes a very different turn in its second half, while The Handmaiden leans much more fully into this theme. The Japanese occupation of Korea works brilliantly as a metaphor for the occupation of sexuality, how it is represented and lived, and also identity. Those layers of foreign language, culture, literature, dress and architecture - who owns them, uses them, aspires to them and are entrapped by them - are all very fine vehicles for examining power dynamics in sexuality and our identity more generally. The architecture is very well used as storytelling device. Small secret spaces are found within the overbearing structure of the old house. Which parts of the house are Japanese, which are English, what happens in which are all loaded with meaning. Sliding doors are used to great effect, whether closed, listened through or opened to let in the light and air and freedom. Gloves do a similar thing. So many have misunderstood this film, too disturbed and distracted by its visual content and themes. But, as others have mentioned here, it’s very much a piece based upon, shaped by and celebrated by queer women. It works brilliantly for them and other marginalised groups and is rightly cherished by them. I’ve watched this with some strident (hetero) feminists who became very indignant and completely missed the point. I’d recommend watching “The Duke of Burgundy” as a related piece. Many similarities between them.
label: analysis
notes: Names source novel (Sarah Waters' Fingersmith), describes how the adaptation diverges; specific comparative claim about the transposition

text: Nah, she was playing it as legit romance at some point. Like...horny wine mom romance. At first it started out super edgy. In the utmost primitive way, but I dig that, so I was kind of excited. Like Verhoeven only dumber. But she's not like Verhoeven, she's more like Eli Roth. Promising fucked up and nasty shit, but delivering none of it. The weird thing was that the movie constantly alternated from clearly not taking itself that seriously, making dick jokes, etc. and going for genuine, albeit toxic, romance. Fennell talked about how hot and bothered their romance made her when she read the book. And that that's the part she wanted to focus on. Like, steamy sadomasochistic lust and passion. None of which was anywhere to be found in the film. Except that one pseudo-kinky scene. I mean at the end, where he's kissing her dead body, crying, telling her she loves her. That was clearly meant to come off as heartbreaking. The thing is that Fennell seemingly never made any attempt to deliver something that would make an audience feel like she did when she read the book. All she did was show what she felt when she read the book. Making it about her instead of whatever is in the book.It's like if she had a bunch of flour, and her idea of sharing it with others was posting pictures online of her eating the bread she made with it. Expecting others to not be hungry anymore looking at it.Maybe that's just something you can't help when you grow up like her. 😄 I think you're giving her too much credit. I don't think she's a competent enough writer or director for it to not be the likeliest explanation she just doesn't know how to achieve what she set out to do. Or maybe she doesn't even have any and she just likes being a director and just says whatever in interviews. Though all the intentionally comedic parts worked well and were the best part of the movie in my opinion.
label: analysis
notes: Interpretive argument about director's evolving tone; comparisons to Verhoeven and Eli Roth as specific directorial analogues with reasoning

text: A good write-up! Curious what you think about The Munekata Sisters, if you've seen it. It's been many years since I did but I found the approach to female characters very different from his other films, not conservative slop. The portrayal of the younger more modern sister is brilliant, and her independence is never "fixed" by a man or judged, it is simply a difference between herself and her sister. Their relationship is very real. Worth a watch. It's clear he lived a deeply closed life, and his art reflects a longing for peace and stasis above all else. He was also, however, a brilliant and empathetic artist, and clearly understood on SOME level that this idyll wasn't realistic or sustainable. So he mostly makes gorgeous portraits of a tragically changing Japanese society, idolizing tradition, while occasionally making a movie that shows the pain and ugliness of that tradition. He knows they're there, but isn't the most comfortable exploring them. He'd rather stick to the familiar. Something I, as a similarly closed off person, can understand. Still the GOAT to me. I recognize his flaws as a person and artist but no one has moved me more with less. Every part of every movie I've seen of his has felt perfect.
label: analysis
notes: Opens as question but makes specific claim about Munekata Sisters: female characters portrayed differently, younger sister's independence as radical

text: It is also an outright critique of the outdated conservative structures and the systemic rot it brings within Japan, which is unlike his other works, where he champions them. In which films do you think that Ozu championed the conservative structures of Japan? I think you may have approached his films assuming that was what they would be about, and as a result read them as supporting the status quo. Tokyo Twilight might just be the first one where you realized that they do not. I would not be surprised if you feel every Ozu film you watch from here on out critiques those structures. Of the 9 films you listed most of them are sympathetic to those who go against the conservative structures of the day, the younger generations, or function as down right critiques of them. The Lion Dance being on that doesn't, and I would need to rewatch What Did The Lady Forget? for me to comment on it more. Early Summer is about a woman going against the societal traditions and taking agency in her life by choosing he own husband. Early Spring was Ozu trying to "portray what you might call the pathos of the white-collar life." Late Spring is a film all about the pain caused to a family by the rest of society forcing them to conform to societal norms (very similar to Late Autumn, but less comedic). An Autumn Afternoon shows the very limited options that women of the day had, and how despite what they or their family may want, while also having some critiques of Japan during the war. Good Morning functions as a send up of those rigid rules and structures that people enforce, showing the chaos that can be caused by even the slightest defiance of those rules, while also comparing them to farts. I often see people make these comment about Ozu's film and it always boggles my mind. Especially when you look the life Ozu had. Late Spring being a great example. I see people say it's a film that supports the status quo, or that shows what Ozu deemed a necessary part of life, but he obviously didn't feel that way. Ozu lived his entire life unmarried, with no rumor of romantic or sexual relationships, living with his mother. It feels quite difficult to look at that and then read films like Late Spring as having a conservative message. All these factors make this film feel quite like a new wave film to me because of its themes of alienated youth, critique of the family system, emotional disconnect and postwar moral decay. As shown in the above films you have already seen, but also very clearly in Late Autumn, The Flavour of Green Tea Over Rice, Equinox Flower, and others, this is a very common theme in Ozu's films. A film like An Autumn Afternoon may seem more conservative in its messaging to one unfamiliar with Ozu's films and life, but it is a film in conversation with the rest of his filmography. One of my favourite take aways from Ozu's films is that there are not "right answers" that work for everybody. You have to look at each individual situation to see what is needed. I haven't read exactly what you are saying about Ozu making Tokyo Twilight, do you have a source on that? From what I've read he found it difficult to make more so as a result of how heavy the subject matter was, rather than because of "succumbing to the demands of the youth", because again, so many of his films (even dating back to the silent era) have much more sympathy and understanding for the younger generations compared to his contemporaries. His return was to less heavy films, not to more conservative films. That's why Tokyo Twilight was followed up by two comedies.
label: analysis
notes: Argues Tokyo Twilight critiques conservative structures unlike Ozu's other works; then challenges the counter-claim with Early Spring evidence

text: Could you be more specific about where you think in his other films he is upholding some sort of structural order? By coincidence I just watched EARLY SPRING (1956). Everyone is pretty cynical and going through the motions of what they have to do in society, but not through any belief or enthusiasm. A widow does not mourn her husband A young woman freely engages in an affair with a married man A married man has an affair, but doesn't appear enthusiastic about his marriage or his affair partner People sit there in their office chairs, doing pointedly meaningless "salaryman" labors Workers talk about management, not with any respect, but all about political advancement People are not happy. I mean, they actually say in the film "everybody today is dissatisfied" and "life is gloomy" and "we are cooped up all day." With the exception of some people in traditional Japanese clothing, everybody is interchangeable in the dress of men and women in 1950s western style I always read that as Ozu saying after the fall of the Japanese Empire there wasn't really an ideology except work and fitting into social norms that took its place. The young woman is probably the most joyful person…But she's not getting any satisfaction out of her relationship or her job. I don't see anything in this film that supports the social order.
label: analysis
notes: Uses Early Spring as specific counter-evidence: characters are cynical, going through motions, not believing in structures they inhabit

text: Just saw this last night. Aside from the directors you mentioned, there are a lot of similarities to Charlotte Wells' Aftersun in its treatment of memory. Memory being depicted on film is a big soft spot for me. However, I actually have the opposite reaction of the two halves of the film as you. The first half I adored. Just the vignettes of the siblings hanging out feels so cozy. Plus, there is something about the twilight of the pre-phone era of the 90's feels so uniquely nostalgic. I didn't dislike the second half, but it felt a little too expository. Upon reflection, I really like the dissonance between the main character's memory of her brother verses the reality that was presented. That said, I wish there was more subtlety - but maybe that's little unfair because I want this film to be another Aftersun.
label: analysis
notes: Comparative analysis: Blue Heron vs Aftersun re: memory treatment; identifies opposite reactions to the film's two halves

text: Just got home from the theater and I'm really glad I saw this. The look and sound of this movie was really grest. Spoilers spoilers...... the inexplicable merging of these two times, 20 years apart, I thought was pretty brilliant and emotionally totally worked on me. 4.5/5

text: Yes! The film’s religion definitely reads as thoroughly material: letters, pilgrimages, the handkerchief, casual invocations of God ectThe letters were the most interesting part of this for me bc they translate personal material suffering into this shared, bureaucratic form, which Dora can then commodify. She’s a strange character in that sense because it’s like she begins outside of this material religion, as a very disillusioned mediator of it. It is definitely about a material, socially produced religion, especially as an ‘inverted consciousness’ (Marx possibly?), with material suffering being turned onto an imagined relation with something beyond it. This disjunction feels very uncomfortable at some points (the singing of the pilgrims comes to mind), but at others it is surprisingly hopeful. When they are writing letters to the saints it feels so hollow and commodified but also more alive and joyful than any other point in the film, like everyone knows on some level? I definitely see Italian neorealism in the idea of solidarity among the lower class winning over doctrine
label: analysis
notes: Specific textual evidence (letters, handkerchief, pilgrimages) used to argue religion in Central Station is material not spiritual

text: I was so let down by this movie. I try to avoid knowing anything about movies as much as possible before going to see them. With Marty Supreme that was difficult since everyone was talking about it but I really only wanted to see it because I saw Tyler the Creator was in it. I didn't know it was the same writer and director as Uncut Gems but pretty early on I started to think it was.There were similar themes like "success at all costs" and gambling, over confidence that can be unfounded, etc, and I think those are fun themes in films. My biggest complaint for Marty Supreme was how little I felt like I could connect to anyone in this story.One of the best things about Uncut Gems is that the characters are both likable and unlikable at the same time. I felt like I cared about them as I watched their lives unfold in front of me. I cried when Howie had his meltdown with his girlfriend not because I thought he was a good guy or deserved redemption but because I know the feeling of trying your best and things not working out and I could feel his desperation. I did not feel anything like that in Marty Supreme. The closest I came to that was the "paddle" scene but it really did not connect the same way for me.I think the concept of the character of Marty is great but the execution felt hollow. I read that Safdie wrote backstories for every character in that movie but I didn't feel that shining through at all. Not that the characters felt unrealistic, just that they all felt unrelatable and boring.The worst part about this is when I try to find reviews of people who agree with me, all I am finding is people criticizing Marty and calling him a bad guy, unsympathetic and a narcissist. I feel like those people are missing the point of the film. I didn't think it was a bad movie, I just wanted more from it.Uncut Gems is one of my favorite movies. There are so many layers to it and it has an almost mystical or spiritual vibe to it along with themes of race, shame, religion, family, loyalty, risk and addiction. I am both rooting for and against Howie the whole movie but there are so many other moving parts happening at the same time! The movie isn't just about Howie. Marty Supreme is just about Marty. Which would be fine if I felt like I could connect with him at all in any way.Maybe this has just been my experience but I love to hear other opinions in agreement or disagreement, I would love to discuss, not here to try to shoot the film down. I personally didn't like it but would love to have my mind changed.
label: reaction
notes: "I was so let down by this movie" — disappointment narrative; personal context (Tyler the Creator) dominant

text: There are dozens of better takes than mine in this thread, but on one viewing, I did develop this theory: Structurally, Marty Supreme and Uncut Gems are very similar, but the endings are inverted: (SPOILERS, obviously): In Uncut Gems, the tension builds and builds and all the crazed long-shot/house-of-cards schemes astonishingly and impossibly pay off at the second-to-last moment. Then, at the actual-last moment, it doesn’t matter. He’s dead anyway. In Marty Supreme, it’s the opposite: All of the convoluted schemes to get out from under life fail one after another. Then, at the last moment, he’s thrown a spiritual lifeline and lives. As I said, all the other takes here are more in depth and insightful, but I think this superficial aspect of the plots was interesting in comparison to each other.
label: analysis
notes: Structural comparison: Marty Supreme vs Uncut Gems with inverted endings; specific narrative claim about what each film does with tension

text: I watched Uncut Gems when it originally came out and think both films are roughly the same quality, even if I personally liked Marty Supreme a bit more. What you say about Uncut Gems, that "there are so many layers to it", I think can be said about Marty Supreme too. While "success at all costs" is certainly a major theme, I feel it is a bit simplistic to reduce Marty Supreme just down to that. In many ways, Marty Supreme seems to me like it is a multifaceted approach to the American Zeitgeist right after World War II, with all its idiosyncracies and complexities (mind you, this is just what it seemed like to me, I'm not American myself). Marty wants success at all costs, yet at times he also treats table tennis more like a spectacle and a performance art rather than a competition. He is boastful and fills his matches with pageantries and theatrical antics, yet at the same time he really wants to prove himself as the best player in the world. At least to me, this feels like America's self-perception right after World War II: the best country in the world, but one that constantly feels the need to prove this not simply through results, but through an extra layer of idolatry. What was impressive to me is that the film really doesn't seem to just look at this in a cynical way. Marty genuinely loves what he does, he loves both the competition and the spectacle, the sport and the art. His final match with Endo (who is presented both as his equal and yet someone completely different) really nails this I think: a fierce, competitive table tennis match that simultenously feels like a dance or a professional wrestling match, with the crowd constantly at the edge of their seats. He wins, and even though it should really be meaningless, to him it feels incredibly meaningful, not only because he proved how good he was, but also because he got to stand on a stage and engross a crowd of two entirely different cultures with an incredible performance. Him hugging Endo after the match and telling him what a great player he is only furthers how much he loves what he does. And I do think this is where another layer of Marty Supreme comes in, that it shows us just how much we are often willing to do for the things we love, not purely out of a drive to succeed, but out of a genuine passion. The old gangster loves his dog so much that he is ultimately willing to die for it, while Gwyneth Paltrow's character traded her passion in for a cushy, secure and normative life that only caused her unhappiness. Neither path is presented as perfect and even Marty arguably loses as much as he gains by the end. The film could have well just presented sacrifice for passion as something universally bad, but I feel like that would have not been nearly as effective as the nuanced look it ended up providing. I also think that Marty's happy reaction at seeing his child was a genuine one, because unlike the endless greed and eventually exploitation that a lifestyle like his can lead to (as seen by Rockwell), Marty seems genuinely content with his win over Endo and how they elevated table tennis and is now ready to turn towards a new passion. Will he be a good father? Who knows, but I also don't think it really matters to the film. I should say that I'm probably a bit biased towards Marty Supreme. I used to play table tennis and quite like watching sports. Hell, I cried during the final table tennis match. But I also think that sports as performance art and the imperfect triumph of passion over greed are core subjects of Marty Supreme that a lof of people miss because they (as you say) wrongfully focus on how "unlikeable" Marty is or, at least I think so, because they compare the film too much with Uncut Gems (which is also a fantastic movie). Besides all of this, I also think Marty Supreme has great editing, a fantastic score and an incredible acting performance by Chalamet, all of which can take the viewer out of the erratic stress of the live of Marty Mauser and into the transcendentalism that many of these seemingly vain attempts at fulfillment actually hold.
label: hot_take
notes: "Roughly the same quality" comparative claim; "so many layers" applied to Marty without citing the layers

text: My take on my dislike of Marty Supreme is that it takes this Mafia mentality of Marty of doing things and finding out without putting a kind of weight to its repercussion or support as if those lies and deception he did would just fly to nowhere. It is not even smart or well planned ruckus. All are accident cascading to him gliding away. I actually argued in my own personal review, that maybe the idea of poverty and maybe its ongoing might be something the Safdies are not familiar. There is a sense of just him trying to run away from responsibility any where he could. This on top of the huge ego trip. The use of Jewishness for moral righteousness. His 'career' as the top table tennis being just him being angry about things not going his way. The two leading ladies treating him like he is the best meat there is all against one dimensional 'antagonist'. A lot of unearned Hero characterization for me.
label: analysis
notes: Interprets Marty's mafia mentality as structural flaw in the film's logic; specific claim about consequences not being weighted

text: I think you just didn't get the movie if you think he was characterized as a hero at any point of the film, the whole point of the film is that Marty sucks, he is a terrible person who evades his responsibilities and has an ego way too big for what he has accomplished. At many points of the film Marty had a chance to stay and play his cards correctly and he would have achieved success that way, The orange ball in real life was a huge success, he threw that away. He was given the chance to actually compete and win at the championship, and he threw that away to win a friendly match. Like everything you said about him are things that are supposed to make you dislike him, but you talk about them as if they weren't clearly intentional. Is it your first time seeing an unlikable protagonist? Also the antagonist wasn't 1 dimensional, he was just a man who you barely get to know because MARTY DOESNT CARE, for Marty he is the person he wants to beat and that's it, he is clearly more complex as you do get to know him (You get to see that he is actually a good sport, he did care about having a real match, etc) but you barely get to do that because Marty never cares to approach him as a person, that's part of the film. This film is not about the cost of greatness is about self-destructive tendencies and delusions of grandeur.
label: hot_take
notes: "You just didn't get the movie" framing; makes claims about Marty's characterization but dismissive opener dominates

text: So I just finished watching this movie. I need to talk about it because it really blew my mind. I didn't know what to expect and honestly I wasn't ready for where the story went. At first it felt like an European thriller. A great surgeon, a mysterious woman locked in his house very classy very calm. Then slowly it starts to show its true self and by the end I was just sitting there thinking... What did I just see. The darkest theme in the movie is mens ego. Robert Ledgard is one of the characters I've seen in a while because he truly thinks he's right in everything he does. His daughter gets hurt and instead of dealing with that pain like a person he turns it into this huge project of control and revenge. The punishment he gives is much worse than the crime that at some point you forget who the original victim was. And that's of the point I think. What really got to me was the Stockholm syndrome part. The captive slowly starts to feel emotionally connected to the man who ruined her life and the director, Almodóvar doesn't show it as weakness. It's more disturbing than that. It's, about survival. It's what people do when they have no choice. That's the part that really stays with you. There's also this mixing of identity, body control and agreement that the movie never lets you feel safe with. Every time you think you've found a moral ground the movie takes it away. It's not a watch but its absolutely worth it. Maybe don't watch it alone at night. Let me know your thoughts.
label: reaction
notes: IJW post; "blew my mind," "wasn't ready for where the story went" — pure reaction to viewing experience

text: I'm so glad to see this thread because it is such an insane movie and it seems like most people barely know about it. I saw it almost by accident, had no idea what it was about, and now I'd say it's maybe the most bizarre story I've seen presented in a relatively normal narrative form (maybe a bit of a soap-opera style), and the wildest part is that it somehow works. If I just read the plot, I'd think it's crazy but would also assume I would just not be able to take it seriously. Somehow this movie, as over the top as it is, made me fully buy into the story. The punishment he gives is much worse than the crime that at some point you forget who the original victim was. The guy never even committed any crime, the daughter just freaked out because she was mentally fucked up due to her mom trauma (the mutilated mom is also a good horror side element to the story.) The dad walked in and thinks he raped her, but she was just having a meltdown. I generally find the physical mutilation horror very effective, even movies that don't take themselves too seriously like Tusk are able to viscerally resonate. What is so genius in this movie is that it is also a very disturbing physical mutilation horror, but the end result (that you see at the start) is a very attractive woman. Where the movie blew my mind was that when I realized who she is, my perception of her genuinely changed and I felt almost a similar horror as if I was watching someone being turned into a walrus or flayed alive while looking at a hot actress. And the scenes where you finally see what happened in the past are legitimately terrifying. Aside from that, the fact that the doctor fell inlove with the guy he thinks raped his daughter the moment he made him look like his mutilated wife (pre-mutilation) was insane but brilliant. So much of how we perceive a person is by what we see. His ex wife no longer looks like herself, the perceived rapist (although Banderas is wrong about the situation) looks like the ex wife, so he really just becomes a better version of her and all of the feelings Banderas had for her shift on to him without even stopping to think how bizarre it all is. As a viewer, you also can't look at her and think she is that guy, but you also stop being able to look at her and think it's just a hot girl. That makes her something disturbing without any need for special effects. The movie starts at the point where you naturally perceive their dynamic as if she was just his wife he is doing some skin surgery on, just to totally shift the perception, that is done so well. i don't even know how it managed to pull it off. I think that the soapy tone kind of helped, being too realistic and serious in other aspects could clash with what we need to accept, but it's still interesting that it didn't create a non-serious tone at all either, I found it psychologically convincing. It's also interesting to think what the guy should do in the end. On one hand he is mutilated for life and could probably kill himself, on the other hand, to the world, he is socially in the best position imaginable.
label: reaction
notes: "Such an insane movie" — excited reaction; "most bizarre story in a relatively normal narrative form" is more observation than argument

text: Gave Gyllenhaal's Lost Daughter a try because I loved The Bride!, and wow I was not disappointed. Powerful critique of women's social pressure to become/be mothers (there is a stark contrast of what is expected of men and expected of women throughout), and that alone would have been good enough for a strong film. But Olivia Colman was incredible (loved her in The Favorite previously), one of the most interesting female characters of her age and psychology I've seen. And the entire end with the cruel selfishness of the stolen doll, and even more her desire to confess or even display her sin was just a remarkable finish to the film. She is revealed as a kind of (lightly?) sociopathic woman capable of careless cruelty, but in a way that reflects onto the moral imperative of motherhood that all women fact, in just a startling way. I watched it tonight and I immediately want to rewatch it tomorrow with a view to watching the whole story unfolding knowing what the end revealed about her psychology and nature, to see if I understand the earlier scenes differently. What a character study and commentary.
label: analysis
notes: "Powerful critique of women's social pressure to become mothers; stark contrast of expectations for men vs women throughout" — specific thematic claim with mechanism

A movie that made me think about my own mother a lot and just see her that much more of a 3D person.
