text: I am so glad to see this -- it's such an unbelievably fascinating movie which takes an uncinematic and unpleasant topic and turns it into a thriller. Or more accurately it paints an entirely internal conflict with the superficial trappings of a thriller when the only 'real' violence is at the very end. After three years the scene that sticks with me the most is when Leda tries to be 'spontaneous' and dances with Ed Harris in the bar but is overwhelmed by self-consciousness; it's not even really tied to the central concept but it's such a great directorial choice to include. Even its flaws are interesting to me. I didn't love the way the flashbacks were used (sometimes heavy-handedly, and although her performance was great it felt like a big ask for us to buy that Olivia Colman and Jessie Buckley were the same woman) but they were still compelling, and it feels like opening with the ending and flashing back was something Netflix insisted on including to promise a conflict. Also, I don't quite think the movie's transposition of the Italian culture of the novels onto the American class system worked exactly right. Eg. in the book, the family on the beach are not just 'rough' and working class but are supposed to seem more ominously linked to the Italian mafia due to their locale of origin (which the movie tries to replicate by making them Italian New Yorkers, but like...it's the 2020s) vs Leda's upscale-ness. But even this is a fascinating 'flaw' to consider.
label: analysis
notes: "Takes an uncinematic/unpleasant topic and turns it into a thriller... paints internal conflict with superficial trappings of a thriller" — specific formal claim

text: Just my response. I can't comment on the book, only the film. I don't think crises are necessarily "obviously" triggered. Sometimes they are just cumulative, and small things can trigger subconscious pressures [edit in: my wife points out that it is when the child disappears that the trigger actually happens, an uncommon event, not to mention the importance of the doll]. I enjoyed the feeling that this was something that just bubbled up in what seemed like a very normal, well adjusted person. It implied that this is something she had long buried and had become accustomed to. Also, I feel like it wasn't just that this was a young mother, but rather that she herself was a particular KIND of young mother, an "unnatural" mother (who lacked a degree of empathy, was quasi-unfeeling, almost sociopathic - too extreme a term, but like that), and this young mother another of the same kind (I read the film as less a story of "any" mother, or all motherhood, though it works as a critique of the wider subject). Well, she was nearly murdered, that can be transformative. Also, it felt like she wanted to confess/share her "sin" with a young counterpart. That felt transformative to some degree (she could have just left the doll somewhere). They were flat, I believe, because they represented the male, Patriarchal gaze, they weren't full characters. Can't comment on the book, only the film. In the film these red herrings really dramatized just how mortal the sin of being a bad mother is. It's like you are the killer of a child, and patriarchal judgement can feel like being hunted down by men. A man can do the very same things, make the same choices, and it is hardly a mortal crime at all (as Ed Harris's character illustrates). Can't comment on the book.
label: analysis
notes: Argues crises are cumulative not obvious; uses specific scene (child disappearing) as the trigger; falsifiable interpretation

text: Upon returning to Barry Lyndon for the first time in years (a truly phenomenal film), I was struck by the complexity of its focus on masculinity, especially the manner in which these traits manifest in an honour culture – honour culture being a type of culture where violent reactions are appropriate in the defense of social slights and one’s image. From memory, I thought the film was mainly a straight satire of these characteristics – and in some sense, it really is. The opening narration is genuinely hilarious: Barry’s father could have been anything if he wasn’t killed in such an absurd manner. The huff-and-puff of the original duel between Captain Quin and Barry is played for laughs, highlighting the absurdity of male of pride. In addition, the passages throughout the war sequences, including the barbaric octagon where men need to prove themselves and the manner in which the war is conducted, all mock the ridiculous rules surrounding male honour. Although, I found upon this rewatching, that its treatment of male honour/assertiveness is paradoxical and provides an extremely nuanced view of how these values can go completely wrong, but also why, at times, they are essential and appropriate at times. I mainly see this from Lord Bullington. A neurotic, introverted child that ultimately seems like Kubrick’s own version of Hamlet, his transformation from his submissive position and his need to reestablish his honour leads to the eventual dispelling of Barry and the reclaiming of his family.
label: analysis
notes: Barry Lyndon honour culture argument; specific claim about how masculine traits manifest in that cultural context

text: This is one of my favorites. It has been several years since I watched it, but thinking on it, there are definitely elements where the masculine energy of the film gets subverted, either by narrative irony where things blow up in someone's face, or by less masculine figures coming out ahead. There are also elements where male characters are depicted in a very feminine way, such as the use of face paint/make up, which seems to largely be associated with the noble class. Lord Bullington at the end, like you mentioned, is pale, pubescent, and indignant in a way that is classicly childish. Either of these depictions, the Chevalier or Lord Bullington, are some of the only characters that come away from the narrative with a positive trajectory. Meanwhile, when the tragedy happens near the end, it echoes the famous image of Napoleon on horseback. There's also a juxtaposition of homosexuality, when Barry steals the horse, and later when he shares a kiss with his dying friend. These moments are subdued but intimate. Completely lacking in spectacle. Compare that with the scenes shot with solely the light of hundreds of candles, or large landscape painting across entire walls, all of which delights in the spectacle. There are so many interesting pieces of the narrative that all feel so deliberate. There are so many layers to this film. For me, it works on every single level in a way that few other movies have.
label: analysis
notes: Identifies specific subversion patterns in Barry Lyndon: narrative irony, less masculine figures coming out ahead; names the mechanisms

text: Yesterday I got myself into a little trouble over at r/movies by posting a rather troll-y bit about how flagrantly masturbatory and circle-jerky the recent reviews of Netflix’s The Power of the Dog were. I copied quotations from certain reviews and gave my thoughts on them. All around I thought it wasn’t a terribly offensive post by any stretch of the imagination (in fact I thought it was a clever format that I never see in that incredibly popular and flawed subreddit), but boy oh boy did I feel the heat for it. Perhaps it was my attitude towards what I thought were really lazy and uninspired replies (a lot of “OP discovers opinions,” and nauseatingly unoriginal variations of that), or perhaps it was the fact that I simply didn’t like what is apparently the greatest film since Parasite or Whiplash, or probably it was just that I said it how it was, which was that the movie sucked and it doesn’t take a lot of film knowledge to see that (simply seeing a movie like McCabe & Mrs Miller is enough evidence for anyone to conclude that The Power of the Dog is a big fat stinker; no education or mental gymnastics required). Either way, after I got banned for a week for getting “into it” with someone who thought that simply because the majority of people disagreed with me that I *must* be wrong, I did some thinking last night. I concluded that, 1) I went about my post incorrectly, and 2) The Power of the Dog is a movie so deceptive and bad that it, ironically, actually does deserve a thorough and real review. So here is my review, which you can bet your butt isn’t paid for in cash like the army of fake experts who have catapulted this film to Best of the Year territory. It’s paid for in blood, and the two hours of my life I’ll never get back from Jane Campion. The Power of the Dog is a film about toxic masculinity, repressed homosexuality, and the gray area between the two. To even begin reviewing this film, we have to first address these two subjects, and determine whether their concomitant relationship to one another (in this film specifically) is acceptable, or “woke,” or transgressive, or aged, or offensive. And, frankly, it doesn’t take much to figure out which. Let us transport back in time briefly to 1999, when American Beauty gripped the nation with its unique take on suburban life, depression, and whatever else that movie thinks it’s about. If you’ll recall, Chris Cooper plays a mean, hard military man who – as it happens – is gay. He is the repressed archetype; a man who is so afraid of his own sexuality that it turns him into a violent, homophobic jerk. Now, it’s easy to see that Phil’s character in TPotD is, literally, the exact same archetype. That’s not anyone’s fault, of course. And besides, the source material for the Netflix original was written way back in the 1960s, long before American Beauty. But what I really want to point out is the trajectory of a film like American Beauty (and others that will be mentioned in due time), and how it will undoubtedly parallel the film in question. You see, the archetype of the gay man who hates himself and his gayness is… well… stupid. And not just stupid, but regressive. This should be obvious to any slightly progressive-minded individual, but for those not aware, I’ll explain it a little better. Homosexuality is an ubiquitous, natural state of being. It’s ancient. It’s referenced in every form of art, from every culture, for as far back as society has left traces in the stone and dirt. People are gay. And, of course, people are ok with their gayness (have you ever met a gay person who was not?). Now, this isn’t to say that people aren’t bullied, or forced away from their families, or – in extreme cases – beheaded for their gayness. But generally speaking, the gay experience is distinctly NOT well represented by the idea that being gay is something many men feel deeply ashamed of. And, curiously, TPotD seems vaguely aware of that, since Peter’s character is more or less openly gay and doesn’t seem to care what people think about it, despite the bullying he receives. What’s more is that, not only does Phil’s character hate and hide his own gayness, but that that repression in turn is what makes him a toxic male. So repressed gayness = toxic masculinity in the world of TPotD. Let’s get that clear. Phil hates himself for being gay, and he projects this hate onto basically everyone, except, quite curiously, his own ranchers, who worship him and are perfectly secure in their own assumed straightness, since they frolic and bathe together openly without anyone calling anyone a “faggot,” an expression they reserve solely for Peter because of his tight jeans (and that’s not a joke, that’s actually how this movie operates). I assume we’re supposed to interpret that Phil feels very uncomfortable about his fellow cowhands’ hetero-security, since he disappears to his own secret pond to swim naked and occasionally masturbate to homoerotic pornography. All of this is very incongruent from a logical standpoint, but more than that, it’s just plain lazy (on top of being politically fraught). Let us ask ourselves: what is the legacy of a film like American Beauty? Well, it won a collection of Oscars, yes. And obviously there is the Spacey drama that has tarnished most if not all of his filmography. But even before that, where does a movie like American Beauty find itself in the 21st century? Still revered? Still on the cutting edge of thematic exploration? Still considered a top film of the 90s, or even just 1999? The answer to all of that is obviously no. American Beauty, as it turns out, is a very controversial film now, and is regarded with nowhere near the same acclaim as any of its contemporary brethren, like The Matrix, The Sixth Sense, Magnolia, Fight Club, or any other wildly popular movie of that year. Somehow, for some reason, American Beauty has simply faded away from the academic or critical lens of cinematic study. I wonder why. Well, to start, the way it handles Spacey’s love affair with the student and the arc of Chris Cooper’s character are both now considered questionable at best, and downright offensive at worst (my focus here is on the latter controversy). Why, you ask? Because the writing of Chris Cooper’s character reveals a staggering lack of knowledge or experience about being gay. It’s actually a villainization of queerness, you see, because (as both these movies state with their characters) if society doesn’t let you be gay, you turn into a monster. Because that’s what men do. That’s what they are. Violent and toxic, and all because of their repression (/s for those who think I’m endorsing that assumption). It should go without saying that this is a very bad faith depiction of queerness. Please ask yourselves, do you know a single person whose own repressed homosexuality has turned them into a monstrous, toxic individual? This is an archetype borne out of society’s already incredibly flawed relationship with queerness. This is the type of snide, childish thinking that we project onto people who drive big trucks, or are vehemently homophobic, or are stoic and lack emotional security. And don’t get me wrong, those people exist! But the reality is that, for 99% of them, closeted queerness has nothing to do with it. To assume it does is to 1) equate queerness as a source of toxic behavior and 2) assume that someone who is queer lacks the courage to address this themselves, and therefore finds it easier to “project” their self-directed disappointment at others. All of this just reeks of ignorance regarding the gay community or gay experience. It’s uniformed, lazy, and rote. Some people are just pricks. And some people are just homophobic. Believing a homophobic person is simply sheltering their own gayness is actually an insult to gayness, I feel. Why can’t that person just be a jerk? There’s no shortage of them on planet earth. Why make any of this about being gay? Now, let me make some things clear before I continue further. There *are* gay persons who have not felt comfortable coming out for one reason or another. And surely, there *are* some individuals who are incredibly toxic and may harbor some closeted same-sex feelings that manifest into violent tendencies. But personally, I find this to be more of a boogeyman than a reality. And my whole point about bringing this up is to highlight that it’s a pattern in Hollywood, and it’s a boring one*. American Beauty* and The Power of the Dog are not alone in the perpetuation of this archetype. Boogie Nights has a character who fits this exact model as well (he even attacks Dirk Diggler after paying to watch him masturbate). And there are others. So the real question is: why does Hollywood insist on perpetuating this silly, tired archetype? I can tell you why. Because critics will praise it. And whether the movie’s legacy survives the natural cultural shifts that occur over time doesn’t matter. All that matters is box office revenue and quantity of views. And critics determine those things by either giving praise or not. Notice how American Beauty is no longer relevant? Hollywood has noticed too. But they don’t care. The money was made. Another film that is ripe for comparison with The Power of the Dog (from a socio-political standpoint) is 2005’s notorious Best Picture winner Crash. I need not elaborate on the legacy of Crash; suffice to say it is a universally hated movie now, despite its initial swathe of accolades. Again, we must investigate how and why this happened. To start, the movie is a pandering mess of implausible gibberish. Everyone is racist, and therefore no one is racist, or something-something. Don’t bother interpreting the film, because its hall of mirrors will only confuse and infuriate you. Just acknowledge the base facts: Crash was a “hot-topic” film that attempted to tackle an array of sensitive topics via the most trite and stereotypical ways imaginable. “Ghetto” black men who end up “doing good.” Nasty Karens who realize not all Hispanics are untrustworthy. A “good cop” whose morals fail at the last moment. A “bad cop” whose morals succeed at the last moment. And on and on, like a snowball traveling downhill through a landfill. Crash is the epitome of everything the average American hates about Hollywood. It revealed them to be out-of-touch, virtue-signaling, isolated narcissists, who think that a movie is relevant and important so long as it addresses “touchy” topics, regardless of whether it has anything meaningful to say about them. What does Crash say about racism? I’ll tell you. Nothing. So why did it win Best Picture? Because it was stunning piece of cinematic craft? Because it spoke to a generation? Because it raised the bar? No. It won because Hollywood is fake, nepotistic, elitist, and run by predators, Chinese businessmen, and Scientologists (which Paul Haggis was, interestingly enough). Crash won Best Picture because we live in a simulation run by people who don’t know what being a real person is. Crash won Best Picture for the same reason The Power of the Dog stands a good chance of winning Best Picture, because Best Picture isn’t about being the best film, it’s about being the most talked about film. Circling back to my initial post regarding this film, I want to address what might have been the most triggering part: my accusation that the reviews for The Power of the Dog are fake. I am genuinely shocked at how negatively that accusation was received. Allow me to confront that head-on. We live in a society that values fakeness. We live in a deeply, deeply corrupt society. I don’t think anyone with a brain would try and argue otherwise. From our politics, to our economy, to our social media, every facet of our lives is riddled with falsehood and invisible machinations that steer our thoughts one way or another. Unqualified people are elected to positions of power. Dishonest corporations are free from paying taxes. Instagram and Facebook spy on us and sell our interests to advertisers, who in turn bombard us with content geared to empty our pockets. Medicine is price-gouged. Police murder people daily. Journalism is dead and has been replaced with click-bait, tabloid-style blogs. Crimes are committed by powerful people who seemingly never have to suffer consequences, no matter how blatant their guilt. And, along with all of the above, media conglomerates conspire to create “hits.” Is it really so hard to believe that a film can utilize a nifty, young PR team to generate hype around an incoming movie? It is so hard to imagine that a critic can be paid to say a movie was good? What about that insinuation strikes anyone as “unimaginable,” when you can look around at our world and see how much truly damaging corruption takes place? So we’ve addressed the possibility of my accusation, and we can leave it where it is. I don’t have proof, which I’m sure really angered the hundred people who ratioed my original post, but frankly, I don’t care. The proof, as they say, is in the pudding. And the pudding, in this instance, is in the weak, toneless-ness of The Power of the Dog. What makes a movie good? There’s no easy answer to this, but there are some good places to start. For one, a movie should look good, which means, more or less, that it shouldn’t look fake. The interiors shouldn’t look like sets. They should look lived in. The props should look natural and naturally placed. The extras should appear authentic. I don’t make professional movies, but I can imagine that this is no easy task. And yet, confident and creative artists have managed this for the most part for basically a century. Something that struck me right away about The Power of the Dog, however, was how remarkably fake the movie felt. Describing this is difficult, because it gets into all types of tedious nuances, so instead I would like to opt for a different approach to really express this sentiment effectively. Take, for instance, the film McCabe & Mrs. Miller, by Robert Altman (which I mentioned earlier, and if which, if you haven’t seen, you must right away). McCabe & Mrs. Miller is famous for many reasons, but perhaps it’s most famous for its incredible world-building, and the authenticity of that world. People often watch McCabe and express that they felt as if they time-travelled. That’s how effective that movie is in coming across as something from the 20th century. Myriad techniques are employed to give off this affect: overlapping dialogue that reflect reality; gritty set design quite literally built from the ground up; and a fluid camera that gives a truly “fly-on-the-wall” perspective of the unfolding events. Another great example of a fascinatingly authentic film (albeit a stylistically very different one) would be There Will be Blood. Another would be Assassination of Jesse James by the Coward Robert Ford. At this point you should notice that I am deliberately picking only movies that tread the same territory as TPotD, which we can safely call “moody Western” or something along those lines. I do not feel pretentious or impudent in the slightest when I say that watching any of those movies side-by-side with The Power of the Dog will make my point here perfectly clear. The others feel real. Dog does not. This doesn’t take a photographer’s eye to notice, although I can see how if you haven’t ever seen a truly quality movie you wouldn’t have a great point of reference here (pardon me if that sounds harsh, but it’s true). Now, some people might say “it’s not fair to compare every movie to classics like There Will be Blood or McCabe; those movies are classics precisely because of how great they are.” To which I will counter by saying that currently, The Power of the Dog shows every indication of being just as esteemed as those movies by critics, and that is a crying shame and worth exposing. If the critics who reviewed this film are to be believed as having truly seen (or enjoyed) it, then I have nothing left but to assume they have never seen a movie that doesn’t look like trash. That, or they are so smitten by New Zealand landscapes that whether the shots have cohesion, poetry, purpose, or symmetry matters not, because “whoa, those mountains are beautiful!” The whole visual aesthetic of Dog is reminiscent of a Clint Eastwood film, where over-saturation and unnecessary sepia filters lazily attempt to insert mood into otherwise dour scenes and events. I’m also reminded of 3 Billboards (another tasteless and politically cringey film), where in the most crucial scene of the movie, the scene where McDormand encounters a wild doe, a fake CGI doe was used instead of a real one. That’s the type of move that just doesn’t fly in the 21st century. Or, apparently it does, but nonetheless deserves to be laughed at. (I mean seriously, a CGI deer? It’s not a gd unicorn, just use a real deer!) It’s not just the look and feel of The Power of the Dog that leaves something to be desired. It’s the tone, which isn’t assisted at all by Jonny Greenwood, who usually composes incredible scores but this time seems to have simply rehashed the disquieting approach he brought to There Will be Blood over a decade ago. It doesn’t work here, because there’s no central emotion to tap into, besides forlorn misery I suppose. I can’t remember the last time I saw a movie that had so little grasp on what emotion it was trying to evoke. I believe that’s why so many of the pretend critics waxed moronically about “harmonious discord” or “unthought thoughts.” There was simply no anchor for anything in the movie. Phil is a miserable prick. Rose is a miserable drunk. George is boring. Peter, the only mildly interesting character, is the heart of the film, and his true intentions aren’t revealed until the very last scene. And it there a catharsis then? Not at all. He murdered Phil. Cool, Phil sucked. Now what? We’ll never know. End of movie. We’re probably supposed to feel some kind of “ahhh” sensation right there at the end, but an “ah” is only deserved when you’ve been well and truly fooled, and for a good reason! Look at The Sixth Sense. Why did it need to withhold its reveal until the end? So that we could understand what the movie was really about: which was finding purpose and becoming complete. Why does The Power of the Dog wait until the last second to reveal what it does? What purpose does the ending serve that changes the way we think about the movie before and after that information? That Peter was “onto” Phil’s toxic ways? That he knew he was gay but thought he deserved to die because of how toxic his repression had made him? Talk about a seriously misguided motivation. Truthfully, I’m not certain what The Power of the Dog was trying to say at the end and why Peter did what he did, but I also don’t really care. No matter what the reality is, it remains uninteresting. The choice seems to be between: repressing your gayness makes you a toxic male who then won’t be able to swoon your love when you eventually find it, or… well, never mind actually, there isn’t more. That’s all this movie is saying, and it’s saying it poorly, which is doubly insulting because it was already saying something stupid. The rest of the movie’s elements are equally amateur. The performances are mildly credibly, except for Cumberbatch’s Phil, which is possibly the worst casting decision I have ever seen. His accent is laughable, and it’s simply impossible to imagine him as any kind of competent rancher. The settings, as I mentioned before, are drab and boring. The irreverent, timeless kind of energy that a film like Days of Heaven utilizes is aspired to and never achieved, despite a budget surely three times the size. The script, which is probably the best part of the movie, is still so vacant and unoriginal that you can’t help but think of ten better movies while watching this one, including all the Westerns I’ve mentioned above, plus the obvious comparisons like Brokeback Mountain or a movie that has something meaningful to say about homoeroticism and toxic masculinity, like The Talented Mr. Ripley. The truth is, every year there is a film like The Power of the Dog. A film that seems spat from an algorithm designed to generate conversations about social topics du jour, but ultimately disconnects from reality with aggressively archetypal characters and no over-arching commentary about society in the slightest. It’s just an equation: toxic masculinity + female director + repressed homosexuality + “ooo New Zealand” = give me an Oscar and rave reviews. And in turn, all the critics who give this film a perfect score get their names right up to the top of the aggregate sites, and they get to relish in the wordiest and most meaningless reviews because to even discuss a film like The Power of the Dog without stating the obvious (that it sucks) you have to perform feats of linguistic magic and invent turns of phrases that, upon closer inspection, say nothing. Much like this film.
label: hot_take
notes: Power of the Dog post: evidence (film quotes) is decorative not argumentative; "circle-jerky" polemic with no falsifiable claim

text: I'll preface this by saying that I don't think The Power of the Dog is a particularly great film, and am in no way jumping to it's defence, but I think your entire review is based on two false assumptions. Firstly, that all gay people are comfortable with being gay. I personally know gay people who loath themselves solely for their sexuality. And that's in the present day, in a western society, where homosexuality is "accepted". Imagine the pressure of being gay in an environment that is not just wholly masculine, but where heterosexuality is enforced fully by law, religion and cultural stigma. The idea that gay people could be comfortably themselves in that sort of environment is ridiculous. The ranch hands make fun of Peter (who is visibly affected by it - being brought to tears at several points throughout the film), simply for being effeminate. Can you imagine the trouble he'd be in if they were to actually find him in the midst of a homosexual act? Secondly, that the goal of a film is to avoid being fake. Quite frankly this is an absurd idea. Some films are based entirely on an aesthetic of fakeness. I don't think this is what Campion is particularly going for here, but this film does feel at home in her ouvre. It's her style of filmmaking, and you not liking it doesn't make it "fake". Film worlds exist within the confines of the artistic approach taken to making them. Does a Wes Anderson film not count as a good film because the sets are obvious? I don't think The Power of the Dog was aiming for realism, it seems to me that Campion was striving for something a bit more poetic. With regard to the negative representation of queerness, I can somewhat see your point, but it's pretty easy to argue that the film is showing the repression of homosexuality as a tragic thing that affects everyone, not just the oppressed. I've seen all the films you referenced to back up your arguments, and I don't really see what you're trying to say by making these comparisons. To be honest, this post reeks of the kind of pretentiousness you'd expect from a sixteen year old film student who has just discovered Altman and thinks they know better than everyone else about something which is in fact, entirely subjective.
label: analysis
notes: analysis around a hot take example

text: Old I know, but I think the true problem with army of the dead's cinematography wasn't inherently the fact it had an extreme shallow depth of field (alot of the complaints like "oh I can't see anything" or "erm, whuddabout the production design" on their head are dumb to me as i am firm believer in obcuring things as long as what something is conveyed to the point of understanding in service to the larger picture but we'll get to how with from what I've gathered with aotd that's neither here or there), but rather, that it squandered the storytelling potential of such shallow depth of field by its effective use, by not using it for any thematic reason that could underscore the feeling of being in a post apocalyptic setting (something ironically enough explored way better in of all things, a dream sequence in Zack Snyder's Justice League), but instead was purely used for Zack Snyder's bastardized rule of cool that permeates so much of his filmmaking. From what I've gathered, there's literally no legitimate reason for the shallow depth of of field, It was done (as Zack said it) to "hone in on the things he likes to look at". Effectively, a self serving, masturbatory experiment with no hypothesis to prove. There was no vision, it was an ugly movie trying to be beautiful and that's where I'm going to make the argument that it could've justified being the way it was by going actually further than what they ended up doing and for an entirely different reason. What convinced me of what I'll say was seeing the vfx breakdown for this movie, where it showed a hard vignette from the canon dream lens as it does do that on the vistavision sensor of the red monstro he used, and I think it works in conveying how I think it would feel to be in such a setting, it had actually mood to me, where seeing the trailer and other released footage didn't have that. It might seem so simple especially when by most measures it's a technical mistake but I believe that for the sake of everything in that movie, it would've been better to actually have intentionally sought out ugliness that even literal technical oversights like dead fucking pixels could've added to it. I think a cinematographer who is a master at that very thing in movies where that was the case and such ugliness added to the mood and elevated the stories like Lars Von Trier's breaking the waves and Danny Boyle's 28 days later was their shared cinematographer, Anthony Dod Mantle. You know about 28 days later in which the camera choice punctuated the vibe of the movie, and you may know how Lars and Anthony worked by intentionally fucking with image by doing things like removing a color channel, transferring the 35mm film to digital to film back when that looked awful in service of elevating the ugly melodrama of it all by making it look ugly and thus conveying the feelings of it. What I'm trying to get at is there was salvation by actually bothering to write a reason by writing with the image to actually convey something instead of doing it for nothing.
label: analysis
notes: Document it in your planning.md as: "analysis can look like a rant; the test is whether removing the film references would collapse the argument. In this comment it would — in the Nolan polemic it wouldn't."

text: I'm sure I'll get sh*t on for this, so let me give you a slight backstory to where I'm coming from. I'm 32, and I fell in love with film when I was about 14 and my mom had one of those Hollywood Video passes. I soon discovered Kubrick and fell in love with his work. I also grew up pentecostal-christian ( I used to speak in tongues) and now am Atheist. So here's my odd interpretation of the film. The stalker is clearly not a good person. His wife begs him to stay with her and their child and he's just emotionless and completely lacking empathy (sociopath?). Then we hear him start to talk about mystical stories, things that defy the laws of nature. Fast forward to when they arrive in the zone and he sends the cart back... then he goes to lay in a field of radiated flowers. Clearly, the guy has some screws loose and is probably not just a sociopath, but an actual psychopath. He may be seeing these delusions inside his head. But wait... maybe he's being truthful? The Stalker states that "You don't go back here. Nobody goes the same way" and talks of the environment changing. Yet the Professor successfully goes back to get his backpack. Then the Stalker takes the Writer in a loop back to the backpack and goes "how is that possible" (delusion). The "meat-grinder" will supposedly un-alive you, yet nothing at all happened when they traveled down this corridor. Further proving that the Stalker is mad. Yes, the Stalker claims Porcupine's brother was un-alived in that hallway. There is indeed bones at the end, but unfortunately there is more evidence against the Stalkers claims than for them. So this dead body could be anyone. To even-further discredit these outlandish stories from the Stalker, you must look at how he contradicts himself. He says that "the room will give you your deepest desires". If Porcupine was so upset about losing his brother, wouldn't that be his deepest desire? To have his brother back? The room would have granted this. MAGICALLY they return back to the zone. This part of the film bothered me quite a bit considering it took 2 hours to get into the zone, yet they easily returned. And how angry is the wife? Did she leave him? No... she's not angry at all. Like nothing ever happened. Then they go to the zone for one day and return (altho stalker said he would stay there with his family forever?), with zero supernatural anomalies. However, the scene at the end is quite the curveball, showing that the child has telekineses. Unfortunately, this doesn't prove that anything the Stalker has claimed is true about the zone and the room. It's basically a random cheap "payoff" for those expecting something supernatural to happen in the film.
label: analysis
notes: Worth documenting in planning.md as: analysis can open with hot_take framing ("I'm sure I'll get shit on") and include personal context without changing its label. The test is what the body of the comment is doing. This one is building an argument with evidence. The attitude is a wrapper, not the content.

text: Aronofsky's Mother! I really really didn't like or care about any of the characters. It's not terrible, I just ended the movie having a very strong dislike of it.
label: reaction
notes: Decision rule to document: the difference between reaction and hot_take is whether the opinion is about the film or about the viewer's experience of it. "This film isn't profound" is a hot_take. "I didn't find the film profound" is a reaction. This comment stays in the first person throughout â€” that's the tell.

text: I agree with the exception of his animated films. His style seems to fit the medium well.
label: hot_take
notes: flag this one as a dataset quality issue, not just a labeling decision.The comment makes a claim about Anderson's films — "his style fits the animated medium well" — that exists independently of the viewer's experience. No evidence, no specific films (Fantastic Mr. Fox? Isle of Dogs?), no mechanism for why the style fits. It asserts rather than argues. That's hot_take by definition.But the more important issue is that this comment is probably too short and context-dependent to be a useful training example. Two problems:First, "I agree" is doing most of the work, and what it agrees with lives in the parent comment. Stripped of that context, the comment barely means anything. A classifier trained on this in isolation has almost nothing to learn from.Second, at roughly 20 words, it's well below the length where your model will be able to pick up on the features that distinguish hot_take from reaction. Very short comments tend to hurt more than help — they add noise without adding signal.Recommendation: set a minimum length threshold for your dataset — something like 30-40 words — and exclude comments below it regardless of how cleanly they label. Document this as a collection rule in your planning.md. You'll have enough labeling headaches with substantive comments without also trying to squeeze signal out of fragments.

text: I'm Thinking of Ending Things Mainly, I was just confused during the whole thing. text: For me, it was The Handmaiden (2016). I love Korean films, and I absolutely loved the other work by Park Chan-Wook (the vengeance trilogy is superb). I liked the plot and how we were able to see the same scenes from different perspectives with different motivations. My main issue is the runtime/repeated unnecessary scenes. It felt kind of fetishy to show repeatedly the lesbian sex scenes when I don't think the audience really gained anything from seeing it again. At some points it felt like it was dragging and I had to force myself not to check out. Don't get me wrong, it's a great movie but I felt like the hype didn't match up to my viewing.

text: I actually like your comment on Bresson’s L’argent because it’s what I admire about his work. text: I was pretty lukewarm on L'Avventura, but I'm not sure I'd put it in the "Just Don't Get" category. There are two films that stand head and shoulders above the rest when it comes to me not getting them, plus one honourable mention - the honourable mention is The Marriage of Maria Braun, which was my second Fassbinder after A Year with 13 Moons, and by comparison to that absolutely stunning film, Maria Braun felt like it could have been his first gig. The next one is El Topo. There's quite a bit to like about it from a cinematography perspective, but its just too much for me. I've never really been into surrealism in any form. Which leads me to the film I bounced off harder than any other: Blue Velvet. Just get it away from me lol.

text: Boyhood is definitely -that- film for me I just didn’t click with it. When the plot finally started to give me something other than non sequitor conversations— it ends. Maybe the abruptness should have made me feel shocked but instead i just thought.. “All of that for that?” Hereditary: Again great acting, good cinematography. And a draggy feeling with a very abrupt ending. I just didn’t find it scary or even tense at all. Just slow. And most recently “Pieles”. Spanish Arthouse movie (with lovely color/art design) that features a girl with a butt for a mouth.. And it wasn’t the subject matter (people with exageratted deformities) that was bad it was the film’s inability to settle on a tone or theme. Too goofy and unclever for the “acceptance” theme it wanted to shove at me.

text: Au Hazard Balthazar: A Man Escaped and Pickpocket are so good mainly because the strangeness of the leads adds to their characterization whereas the strangeness of Marie et. al. mostly alienated me from inhabiting their mental space. Metropolis: Boring, overlong, plot doesn’t follow particularly well (maybe this last one is because I wasn’t paying attention due to my first criticism). I like silent movies and I like Lang (both his European and Hollywood stuff), but this one sucked. Effects were cool for the time. That’s about it.

text: I find all the praise Journey To Italy gets very baffling. Really felt like nothing special, with a very forced 'feel-good' ending. I don't get why Ozu gets so much praise either. I actually appreciated Tokyo Story more after re-watching, but all of his other films seem to be essentially the same. About a single woman who doesn't want to marry and then her parents arrange a marriage to her but she doesn't want it. Even some of his works are a remake of his earlier films (I Was Born, But.. - Good Morning and A Story Of Floating Weeds - Floating Weeds) Both Ivan The Terrible films bored the hell out of me and felt more like theater plays rather than films. The lighting was incredible but I honestly can't see any other redeeming quality.
label: hot_take
notes: "All his other films seem to be essentially the same" — categorical claim about Ozu without naming which films or what they share

text: I am only here to slightly defend the scene in question from The Conformist as a commentary on Italian fascist sexism and how the main character is trying to conform to those ideals by emulating the enforced power of his wife’s assault. It is disgusting and I think that’s the goal of the scene. In my personal reading though.

text: I hate Cassavetes films. I get its influence with Scorsese's and the New Hollywood group but it is just not good on its own. Practically has the most insane rambling I have seen in films, weirdly sexist, interesting characters that never seems to be in the same tangent, AND most importantly, very boring visually. [Note, I had watched Gloria, A Woman Under the Influence, Husbands , Love Stream, Mikey and Nicky, Husband]. I, also , hate almost all of Hughes High School films. The fact that people consider this as the high school film that they most relate to is just insane. I am not American but this are fucked up stories, too convoluted to even happen, character so rotten and self indulgent. I just do not understand. How is this liked? On top of things, a lot of things did not age really well too. [I only like Something Kind of Wonderful from his set] Mostly De Palma's Late Seventies to Early Eighties schlock movies. Not a big fan. His works has aged the worst, story wise. And visually, his works lack the production of his contemporaries. [Sisters, Carrie [I get its charm though acting wise], "Body Double" (1984) , Dressed to Kill**"** (1980)]. I love Phantom of the Paradise though. Random films that are acclaimed that I do not like: Tarkovsky's Solaris and Stalker > Too self serious for its own good. The pacing can make you sleep. The story though is very interesting. Cache and Amour > I see why it is acclaimed but it is just nothing special. Does not really merit its "acclaim" for my eyes Blow Up, The Passenger, La Notte and L'eclisse > I personally do not get this period of Antonioni. I guess I did appreciate the movie about nothing concept that he popularized. Again too self serious for its own good. Visually interesting but the pacing for this one is mind numbingly slow. The story is also uneventful. I appreciate Zabriskie point though
label: hot_take
notes: "I hate Cassavetes films" + "weirdly sexist, interesting characters never in the same tangent" — asserted without scene evidence

text: At the forefront of my mind because of another thread, but I’m going to say Citizen Kane. However maybe for the opposite reason, that I get it completely. I don’t understand what sets this movie above any other “Money doesn’t buy happiness” or idealization of innocence movies. Yes it’s very technically proficient and the pace was something fresh in 1941 but I don’t really understand it’s lasting legacy. I’d call it very par for the course as far as movies we still talk about 80 years later go. There’s a mysticism surrounding it I don’t feel it deserves. It’s all very literal and clear. He was happiest when he was a kid. Sure, weren’t we all? I passionately agree with your stance on Last Tango in Paris. I’d counterpoint your stance on the Chantal Ackerman movie that her prerogative was never to create entertainment nor create something resembling what we consider “a movie”. She had something to say and determined a movie camera was the best tool to express it and wasn’t concerned with how the audience would contextualize it against other movies.
label: analysis
notes: Asks what distinguishes Citizen Kane from other "money doesn't buy happiness" films; acknowledges technical achievements but questions narrative distinction

text: I wouldn’t exactly say I don’t “get” it, but I’ve never understood the reverence for Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind. I know I’m in some kind of hardcore minority among us film lovers, but I can’t think of a more pretentious film that feels so focussed on reaching the climax of the masturbatory direction that it not only forgot to connect with its audience, but actually alienated it. Again, that’s some harsh criticism from me, and it’s just my opinion and I don’t need anyone to explain it to me or tell me why I’m wrong - film school did plenty of that for me already lol. I just feel so painfully disconnected from that film and was left wondering if people were pretending to love it because it was so inexplicably bad that they feared it might be brilliant and expose them as being dumb. Pretty sure I’m the dumb one...and I’ve given it a second chance...maybe in another 5-10 years I’ll give it another go and see how and if it resonates for me then.
label: hot_take
notes: "Most pretentious film that feels so focused on reaching the climax of the masturbator..." — bold characterization, no specific evidence

text: Oldboy. I really liked the first act before he escaped but after that it just felt like it was edgy for the sake of it. Really felt like one of those bad anime where the villain just goes on and on about how brilliant he is with a big dumb smirk. And the hallway fight was just okay to me. I think the reputation for that scene made it really hard for me to ever be satisfied no matter how good it was. I also thought it was weird how he was able to beat them and all the guys in the elevator with no training. I'm fine with the cool factor though so it didn't bother me that much. text: There are some issues with the writing of his movies. He tends to overvalue complexity over clarity and proper emotional effect. So you often end up with a very impressive jumble of plot points that seems smart, so you don't say it's bad, but you don't emotionally process it or react to it because you don't actually know what's going on so you forget the movie not long after you see it. He also has a tendency to create villains that overshadow the hero instead of bringing out the strengths of the hero, and has the villain just get randomly killed in the end instead of losing because of their own villainous traits, and that type of random killing of the villain makes the story much less satisfying for audiences.

text: The absolute master casting of Jimmy Stewart is what seals this film for me. text: Similar reaction to this film. There are only two films I've seen in my life that I can say I connected with so deeply that watching them was a physical experience, like a body blow. One was Andrew Haigh's Weekend, the other is The Piano Teacher. This film literally sent me to therapy. My connection to it set an alarm ringing in my head - you are not OK! That's when I went to therapy for the first time. And yes, admitting that feels uncomfortable, because Erika is such an extreme character. She's also a sexual pervert. I probably wouldn't talk about my connection to this film outside of Reddit. I won't start writing about the film because I've done so many times in the past, and nobody needs that particular wall again. To your point on the ending - I love your thought that her body is her only source of release. I guess this is the second time we see her cut herself for the release. I'd add the possibility that this is a positive ending for Erika. She's alive, and we know that as she leaves the concert hall she will survive. Her life will carry on as before. In some ways this is a triumph. At least she has this - her suffering. It's private and ineffectual, hidden from the world. But it's hers and she'll continue to bear it. To me she is like an iconic religious martyr. The self wounding at the end is framed that way by Haneke - Erika is central against a symmetrical background - the composition is very powerful, like a religious icon. And that's how Erika suffers, like one of the great martyrs. To me it's one of the all time great final scenes in cinema.
label: reaction
notes: 297 words of first-person emotional narrative: sent to therapy, body blow, won't talk about this outside Reddit — dominant register is personal experience despite one structural observation

text: This movie has the ability to summon images / feelings/ memories from a part of your life that seem something from both your / past and your present. I still haven’t figured this one out.
