notes: First-person throughout: 'summon images/feelings from my life', 'I still haven't figured this one out' — experiential response, 35 words above minimum

text: For me, definitely the latter. I'm not a believer, but I don't know how to explain it as anything but a religious experience. It was the same feeling I had walking into the Pantheon. Or when I was with my dog on an otherwise empty beach at 6am and we saw a pod of dolphins swim by not 50 feet away. The feeling that everything is everything. I am not more important or special than anything else. You, my dog, the dolphins, the ocean. But also, I'm not any less important or special. It's a very rare and fleeting feeling. On a side note, when I saw it in theaters, as the credits started to roll and I was wiping tears away, the guy behind me stood up and said at full volume "Well THAT sucked!" Which annoyed me in the moment, but now I think is kinda funny
label: reaction
notes: 149 words of first-person religious/experiential response: Pantheon/dolphins comparisons, theater anecdote — entirely viewer experience register

text: Let's put it like that, when I was a teenager, I thought Saving Private Ryan was milion times better than Thin Red Line, now after rewatching both many times, I think Thin Red Line is milion times better 😁 the scenes with the tribe wake something in my heart every time.
label: reaction
notes: Personal viewing evolution from teenager to now; 'wake something in my heart every time' — first-person emotional, not argumentative

text: Private Ryan is an incredible first twenty minutes (albeit heavily indebted to Robert Capa visually) followed by two hours of generic war movie stuff, topped off by a typically Spielbergian sentimental bookend. The Thin Red Line is on another level altogether, a beautiful and profound meditation on Man and nature that’s moving in a much more complex way.
label: analysis
notes: Specific structural breakdown: 'incredible first twenty minutes' then 'generic'; 'heavily indebted to Robert Capa visually'; 'typically Spielbergian sentimental bookend' — falsifiable

text: That's it, most war films pretend to be anti war, but somehow in the simple fact of portraying the war they end promoting war, whereas the Thin Red Line message manages to be anti war while still portraying it.
label: analysis
notes: Specific thematic claim: Thin Red Line achieves anti-war while portraying war, unlike the genre broadly — argument collapses without this film reference

text: I remember seeing this in the theater and being devastated by the end. My friend and I had opposite reactions where he was disappointed it wasn't a more conventional 'war' film with heroes and villains. There's even a moment where a soldier ponders through voiceover how different his enemy is from him. Just a hell of a good film and one of my all time favorites. The score is also one of Zimmer's best but rarely gets talked about.
label: reaction
notes: Theater experience, devastation, friend anecdote dominate; scene citation illustrative not argumentative; 'one of my all time favorites' is personal register

text: I like to say that while it's not necessarily a war film, it's the best film about war I've ever seen. The sequence where they finally overrun the Japanese CP is breathtaking and horrifying and beautiful all at once.
label: hot_take
notes: 'Best film about war I've ever seen' — bold superlative; scene cited is described experientially ('breathtaking and horrifying') not analytically; no structural argument

text: I saw that one in the theater back when it came out. I don't think I'd seen the original all the way through as a teenager so I didn't have anything to compare it to. I thought it was OK at the time. It was nice seeing James Remar. I've rewatched them both since then. I still think the GVS version is decent but it doesn't come close to that magical level of the original. That one is a 5/5 all day.
label: reaction
notes: First-person viewing history throughout: saw it as teenager, rewatched, opinion evolved over time; 'I still think' framing is personal register not film argument

text: I thought it was a great big failure for all your reasons, and a cash-grab gimmick. That’s a cynical take but I think cynicism and self-indulgence was at the heart of this. It doesn’t feel like he challenges himself. Either copy it *exactly* or not at all, replicate the limitations of the production tech of the time, and make the single change of dropping the expository speech at the end. Make the anachronisms give it an atmosphere of eerie timelessness instead of lazily letting them feel out of place.* I have zero respect for it and it deserves to be forgotten.*Edit: Even then it would be a pointless exercise.
label: analysis
notes: Specific prescriptions for the remake: 'drop the expository speech at the end', 'replicate the limitations of the production tech' — argument cites specific scene/tech choices; collapses without them

text: It’s an incredible work of visual art and such a fun, wild ride. I love the cheekiness of the dude pulling out a longer chainsaw and it just keeps coming. The 80s metal meets 60s psychedelia influences. The horn of abraxos. I mean, cmon. Legendary.
label: reaction
notes: First-person enthusiasm: 'I love the cheekiness of the dude pulling out a longer chainsaw' — first-person response to specific scenes; 'I mean, cmon. Legendary' is exclamatory not argumentative

text: Favorite point, minus all the awesome bits everybody listed? The meltdown over the vodka midway through. Easily one of the greatest moments of anger in film. Not a single bit of dialogue said. It's hilarious, and then, you're like "where are we goin' with this." In classic fashion, the answer is "it's goin' down."
label: analysis
notes: Cites specific scene (vodka meltdown midway through) + specific technique ('not a single bit of dialogue said') as evidence for 'greatest moments of anger in film' claim; film-reference removal collapses it

text: I just love the insane display of obscure references in it. From the Helter Skelter-like sect to the heavy metal subculture. The color palette is nothing but extraordinary, like some mesmerizing merging of biometrics and psychoanalysis. Insane. There some 'romantic horror,' too, but Mandy's overarching concept is that of a revenge tragedy, conflating with various cosmic/urban horror themes. Let it suffice to say that those into Dennis Cooper literature will feel quite at home while watching Mandy. I think it's not that far-fetched to posit that the experience of watching this movie for the first time is up there with Blade Runner and Terminator II.
label: analysis
notes: Genre classification (revenge tragedy, cosmic/urban horror); names specific cultural references (Helter Skelter-like sect, Dennis Cooper literature); Blade Runner/T2 comparison — all film-specific and falsifiable

text: It made my top 5 of the past decade. And yes, it is absolutely case evidence that art is subjective. Some movies I go out of my way to make a case for, others, well, it's in the eye of the beholder, and I don't have any feelings one way or another if anyone says they don't like it. God knows it's not a perfect film, is filled with flaws, as far as parts of the acting, script, pacing... it has such a particular and peculiar aim... but it's so absolutely unique, and it takes me to that transported place outside of thought and analysis in a way that only my favorite films do. I don't know how much I care to interpret it. I don't even know what 44 means. But it provided me that temporary release from the bondage of self, and for that I'm grateful.
label: reaction
notes: Explicitly disclaims analysis ('I don't know how much I care to interpret it'); 'transported place outside of thought', 'temporary release from the bondage of self' — entirely experiential register

text: yeh i too cherish mandy. finally something w interesting visuals. the music is great. story actually has a heart. cool filmnerd homages and nerdy pennsylvania vibes. i like how it goes jus far enuf past revenge to be interesting its too bad the composer passed so young, very talented guy
label: hot_take
notes: 'Finally something w interesting visuals', 'story actually has a heart' — vague quality assertions; no specific scenes or techniques cited to support claims

text: Modern classic. The shots, the dread, especially the score. johan johansson is one of the greatest to ever do it and his other work is worth checking out. Adds so much. Sicario is another one of my favorites with his stuff in it. Really wish he could've done more metally stuff like Mandy. RIP Honestly though I don't love Nick Cage in this, and normally I do, but Mandy feels like he's operating at 80%. Every other member of the cast outshines him, particularly the cult guy and Mandy too. I'm not well versed so maybe a director thing. A better actor or Cage firing on all cylinders and Mandy would be one of the greatest of all time, and it still is in spite of him
label: analysis
notes: Specific performance comparison: Cage 'operating at 80%', cult guy and Mandy character outshine him — falsifiable by watching; Johansson/Sicario cross-film comparison

text: The only war movie I've seen that comes close to the same moral and emotional heft is Paths of Glory, which was also banned in France when it was released I believe. I last watched All Quiet... decades ago and some of the scenes are still etched on my memory.
label: analysis
notes: Names Paths of Glory as comparator for 'moral and emotional heft'; cites historical fact (banned in France) as supporting evidence; both are specific and verifiable

text: I haven't seen All Quiet but I remember being pretty blown away by Das Boot, just on the way it portrays the pointlessness of struggle. I'll have to watch All Quiet, I think it would be interesting to compare the two perspectives.
label: reaction
notes: Personal viewing memory: 'I remember being pretty blown away by Das Boot'; first-person throughout; expresses intention to compare — viewer experience not film argument

text: Das Boot is incredible for that exact reason. It doesn’t really glorify anything it just traps you inside the exhaustion and inevitability of it all. All Quiet hits a similar anti-war feeling, but from the perspective of very young soldiers being psychologically destroyed almost in real time.
label: analysis
notes: Specific comparative claim: Das Boot 'traps you inside exhaustion and inevitability' vs. All Quiet's 'perspective of very young soldiers being psychologically destroyed almost in real time' — both falsifiable

text: I had the same experience of feeling completely removed from what I was watching. Nolan seems to try to make most of the dramatic core be in the Downey Jr. character Strauss’s antagonism. Even though Strauss gets humiliated or given a dressing down by Rami Malek and Einstein and looks like a loser, I think the viewer is still supposed to grant some of his charges of narcissism against Oppenheimer. The structure of the film sort of positions them as grand antagonists reminiscent of The Prestige. I don’t know if biopic films can still get away with doing the composite thing. I do think that it has begun to be looked at as something that, fair enough you can try to do it to make things easier, but it really will be looked at something that the film is ‘trying to get away with’. I just think that that’s where sentiment is at the moment, and Nolan’s decision to approach Oppenheimer in the way he did, is evidence of this trend.
label: analysis
notes: Analyzes Strauss as antagonist and how viewer is positioned re: narcissism charges; compares dual-antagonist structure to The Prestige; discusses biopic composite convention — all film-specific

text: I think Nolan is probably more of a glorified Michael Bay (they have a lot in common), and that he doesn't understand the serious subjects he tackles. It's big, thunderous spectacle and pure entertainment without much substance. And that's perfectly fine.
label: hot_take
notes: 'Glorified Michael Bay' comparison; 'big thunderous spectacle without much substance' — broad filmmaker characterization with no specific films or scenes cited

text: Yes, that's true, it's probably because he borrows stylistic elements from other, greater directors. So we compare him to these great directors, of whom he isn't one. But let's say Nolan remains a Michael Bay-esque director with a classy English accent, and that applies not only to his filming style but also to his themes.
label: hot_take
notes: 'Borrows stylistic elements from other, greater directors' — vague filmmaker characterization; no specific directors, films, or scenes named as evidence

text: To me, the film whilst being a biopic really isn’t. It’s instead a portrait of the men who created the nuclear bomb and how that got turned into the atomic bomb project. All done by highly intelligent people but ones who also were “off” and possibly sociopathic. People who took huge risks that they didn’t know would’ve killed us all, and how that mentality continued through to the Cold War and the MAD doctrine. Oppenheimer is the person we focus on this since we was such an important figure in that initial development, but this is why the film is doing both the nuclear bomb and the atomic bomb timelines with them interspersed. This is why it doesn’t really hit as a biopic and why it avoids all of those tropes. Instead it humanises these figures that otherwise we all too easily see as faceless scientists who were just good at doing science, and not heavily flawed people that made the world so much more dangerous.
label: analysis
notes: Identifies dual-timeline structure (nuclear bomb vs. atomic bomb), explains why film avoids biopic tropes, names specific historical figures and their roles — structural analysis is falsifiable

text: The problem for me with Oppenheimer is that the original high stakes that propelled Nolan to adapt the screenplay (that there was a chance the experiment could have destroyed the earth) are never really felt. We know the outcome, and everyone in the film plays it cool. Even when Cillian is portraying frustration, or anxiety, it's a Nolan-cool sort of performance. Watch Michael Mann's The Insider for example and you'll see a much better performance of anxiety and pressure from Crowe who only has his pension to lose.
label: analysis
notes: Cillian Murphy's 'Nolan-cool' performance vs. Crowe's anxiety in The Insider — specific cross-film comparison; stakes argument (world-ending vs. pension-only) is precise and verifiable

text: The short answer is he has become more of a Movie Mogul than a film director. His earlier films are largely passion projects. Almost everything since 2010 are more product than stories he seems inspired by. It’s a Spielberg product, same cinematography, same tone, often same actors or actors everyone knows. It’s great for the studios bottom line I suppose, but not very engaging as a viewer. I was excited for Fablemen’s but the end result was a jumbled narrative that really didn’t know what film it wanted to be.
label: hot_take
notes: 'Movie Mogul than a film director' — sweeping characterization; 'same cinematography, same tone' without naming specific films; Fablemans critique is vague ('jumbled narrative')

text: I always felt from TinTin onward Spielberg has mostly been doing projects that feel much closer to his heart. Not saying that sentimentality or personal passion isn't something he did in the past (movies like Schindler's List, Saving Private Ryan and Munich are a testament to that) but it definitely feels like he doesn't particularly care about having a hit over doing something that he enjoys making. Even Ready Player One, no matter how you feel the quality of that movie is, shows Spielberg trying to reconnect to his past. But at the end of the day I don't think he's aiming for broad appeal anymore, just making movies he'd like to see and if they make money they make money.
label: analysis
notes: Maps Spielberg career trajectory through named films: Tintin, Schindler's List, Saving Private Ryan, Munich, Ready Player One, West Side Story, Fablemans — argument is verifiable against those titles

text: I think Bridge of Spies was phenomenal. I have a soft spot for espionage flicks and this hit most of the right notes for what I look for in that genre. It maybe was a bit sentimental toward the end (aren’t all his movies) but I hope it eventually has a reappraisal as I feel it deserves to stand out as a strong moment in an otherwise dull decade for him.
label: hot_take
notes: 'Phenomenal' quality claim hedged as personal soft spot; 'hit most of the right notes for what I look for' is personal genre preference, not film analysis; no specific scenes cited

text: I know we’re grading Spielberg on a different scale, but I think like 98% of directors would sign up for the level of commercial and critical success Spielberg had in the 2010s in a heartbeat. This is purely personal preference and has absolutely nothing to do with the artistic merit of the films, but Ready Player One and Lincoln are two of my favorite Spielberg films to revisit, and for wildly different reasons too.
label: reaction
notes: Explicitly disclaims artistic analysis ('purely personal preference, has absolutely nothing to do with artistic merit'); names Ready Player One and Lincoln as personal favorites — experiential register

text: Good question, and it's interesting you cite the 2020s as a comeback when West Side Story and Fablemans did poorly at the box office. Critically acclaimed yes, but it adds a layer to whether it's really what people want to watch. Reading a few articles cited in the Fablemans wikipedia, it seems that "prestige" film is on the downturn for audiences. For me personally I don't really connect with Spielbergs almost sappy kind of feel-good tales anymore. That's all I saw in ads for Fablemans. West Side Story was a similar nostalgia-fueled romp through memory lane wrapped up in modern production values. That's possibly not what you want to discuss here as I'm sure they were technically good movies, but i wonder if lumping his output into decades is an accurate representation of his evolution as a director. Seems to me like he's rather consistent, as he either teeters on wide-eyed fantasy/sci-fi adventures or dramatic period pieces aka the "prestige" films that win oscars. To me his style has always been a bit commercial, but in a way that's completely his own and today feels like a template. That mostly why I'm not for saying the 2020s is a return to better form, but I'm not a huge film nerd just a random guy so..
label: analysis
notes: Uses box office data (West Side Story/Fablemans underperformed) and 'prestige film on the downturn' argument; characterizes West Side Story as 'nostalgia-fueled romp' — specific claims verifiable

text: It's like he was pandering to his past. He made great films that propelled blockbuster filmmaking and brought a lot of technical advancements in cinema, he might have felt he had to continue doing that. So he made films like Tintin and Ready Player One. He was also famous for making inspiring stories based on true events, so he made Bridge of Spies and Lincoln. And these movies are fine, I loved Bridge and Lincoln but it felt like he was going through the motions. All of them were proficient but none of them became memorable compared to his earlier works. I think now he's past trying to deliver hits and just making what he wants. West Side Story and The Fablemans were better than any of his 2010s works.
label: analysis
notes: Career narrative with named films as evidence: Tintin, Ready Player One (technical pandering), Bridge of Spies, Lincoln (passion but going through motions); comparative: West Side Story/Fablemans > 2010s

text: I mean, not every single piece of work someone makes can be an absolute masterpiece — the Beatles have a lot of songs that aren’t Let It Be… but you know what? Those other songs are pretty damn good. You feel me? Many people consider ET to be the best movie ever made… so your point is these others aren’t the best movie ever made? Ok… hahah.. only one movie can be that. The movies you list as a “tier below” his classics are still acclaimed movies that are nominated for many Oscars, and winning some too.. That’s… a very big success. War Horse was still nominated for Oscars including best picture. It’s no slouch. Lincoln is acclaimed, again nominated for a ton of Oscars, and DDL won with absolutely stunning performance. That movie will be remembered for a long, long time. So that’s a monster success. The Post, again, another acclaimed movie, Bridge of Spies another Oscar winner. So what are we doing here? Spielberg’s 2010’s would still be a HOF career for anyone else.
label: analysis
notes: Beatles analogy; uses Oscar nominations for War Horse, Lincoln, DDL's win as specific evidence; 'Spielberg's 2010s would be HOF career for anyone else' — evidence-backed comparative claim

text: The man made The Terminal. One of the most powerful directors of recent decades and he chose to make a waking commercial drenched in product placement and filled to the brim with mawkish sap. That's his emotional core, as a filmmaker, and we're lucky he escaped that occasionally to make a few decent films in his lifetime.
label: hot_take
notes: The Terminal named but not analyzed ('mawkish sap', 'product placement' are broad characterizations); 'we're lucky he escaped that occasionally' — sweeping filmmaker dismissal

text: I think, by and large, you'd be able to see a Spielberg frame and notice it was his. Even in content. I think it's a little more radical and pointed than we make it out to be. Munich is preeeeetty odd. Lincoln could have been a biopic but is by and large eschews conventionality by making it about a specific time period (take away that last shot and it's a great film.) Empire of the Sun isn't a paint by numbers crowd pleaser. Even Close Encounters isn't all light and alieny. It's ending is the definition of sublime and conflicted imo. I think Scorsese is Spielberg's 'arthouse equivalent/ rival' (by fans. I'm guessing they'd get along pretty well as people) but even Scorsese doesn't stick to an exact theme every time. Hitchcock started off making comedies and thrillers, but I think pigeon holed himself into making only murder movies. I like Marnie but it doesn't have the same power as Strangers on a Train. Dunno. I'm just trying to say that I honestly think Spielberg is in a way underrated, and I'm just trying to appreciate his gifts while the guy's still alive.
label: analysis
notes: Names Munich, Lincoln ('specific time period' framing, 'take away that last shot' = scene reference), Close Encounters ending; Scorsese/Hitchcock comparisons with named films (Marnie vs. Strangers on a Train)

text: I want to emphasize that I do not want to start a hate train on this movie. I actually thought it was really good. I'm not a cinephile but I was immersed in the movie. But there was something about this movie that struck a specific chord with me that I couldn't deal with. I usually like horror. I wouldn't say that I'm a big horror fan, but I go and see horror movies with good reviews and usually like them. For this movie, I had to leave before it ended. I've never done that, at least at a theater. I read a lot of the reviews for this movie, and a lot of people mentioned how they got chills, how unsettling it was, the uncomfortable feelings they got from it. I also got all of those, but usually when I watch horror movies, there is also this undertone of excitement. This time I kind of just felt a sinking feeling the whole time. And this isn't meant to knock on the people who enjoyed it because, like I mentioned, it was a well executed movie(unless you enjoyed it for weird reasons). Like I said, I'm not looking to hate on the movie, it was probably just not for me! I'm just looking for people to relate to and discuss with. I'm having a hard time articulating how I feel about this movie. I also feel like this can be a touchy subject which is why I'm over explaining.
label: reaction
notes: Left the theater — 'I've never done that'; 'sinking feeling the whole time' vs. usual horror 'undertone of excitement'; explicitly first-person and struggling to articulate; no analytical claims

text: Frailty is such a thought provoking thriller. It presents itself as a mental health/generational trauma drama. It's honestly kind of predictable for like 90% of the film, but it's enjoyable to see because the actors allow you to experience the religious trauma that leads to an eventual serial killer. It's interesting to watch, with certain aspects of the father/son relationship feeling strangely relatable. You know the dad is insane, but anybody who's had complex family dynamics knows how hard it can be to talk to a loved one about their mental instability. There's also a powerless feeling where even when Fenton wants to turn in the Dad that he might be murdered himself. It succeeds at portraying an understanding but critical look at how a person with a good head on their shoulders succumbs to madness. And then the ending happens. It turns out everything that the dad has been saying the entire movie was true. He really does get visions from God. Him and his son are not only actually brutally murdering demons, but God is helping them on their mission. Now the serial killer son is 100% an actual demon and the empathy felt for his situation is turned on its head. Its such a thought provoking ending. At first I was a bit taken aback that the mental health/generational trauma centered movie performed a 180 out of nowhere, but it has slowly added to my appreciation of the movie the longer I've sat with it. Sure, the dad was objectively correct, but that's almost more horrifying than the initial mental health/generational trauma angle the film was setting up. God is using human beings like pawns to basically act as serial killers. Its as if the craziest cult you had ever heard of that looked like it was the byproduct of untreated mental illness was actually true, and that's more horrifying than had it been mental illness.
label: analysis
notes: Genre classification (mental health/generational trauma drama); character analysis (Fenton's powerlessness, Dad's madness); analyzes ending's 180-degree thematic reversal — all specific to the film

text: Frailty is one of the best and most assuredly under seen and under discussed horror thrillers. The script is masterful, and the cast is firing on all cylinders. I won’t say it’s underrated. It’s not. Most everyone who sees it acknowledges that it is top notch. But it definitely deserves to be brought up way more often than it is. It’s a shame Paxton never helmed another one, and we lost him as an actor too early as well. I guess he’ll go down with other famous single film directors. Laughton w/ Night of the Hunter, Walter Murch with Return to Oz (so SO strange), Brando’s One-Eyed Jacks, and Gary Oldman’s Nil by Mouth.
label: analysis
notes: Places Paxton in tradition of single-film directors: names Laughton/Night of the Hunter, Walter Murch/Return to Oz, Brando/One-Eyed Jacks, Oldman/Nil by Mouth — specific and verifiable filmographies

text: This was a brilliant, depressing movie. I loved that it fully subverted my expectations and that i didn't see it coming. It's pretty rare for that to happen, and when it happened i was very much 'Holy shit'. It's on my watchlist for 'some time soon'.
label: reaction
notes: 'I loved that it fully subverted my expectations', 'Holy shit' moment — first-person surprise response; no claims about how or why the subversion works technically

text: Visually stunning and psychologically haunting movie. Got the pleasure of seeing it on the big screen at the Berkeley Art Museum and Pacific Film Archive 8 or so years ago. Falling into a pit of sand is such a perfect metaphor for the male fear of settling into a life of domesticity. My favorite visual sequence was the shots panning across the woman's body against scenes of the hilly sand dunes. Something about that stuck in my mind for a long time!
label: analysis
notes: Specific metaphor interpretation: sand pit = male fear of domesticity; names specific visual sequence (shots panning across woman's body against sand dunes) — both claims are film-specific and falsifiable

text: Excellent movie, Teshigahara is one of the greatest directors period. Abe is also a great author, and the original novel is worth reading. I highly recommend all three of his other collaborations with Abe, Pitfall, Face of Another (my second favorite), and The Man without a Map. All 4 films are masterpieces or near-masterpieces. I think most can be found on the Criterion ChannelHis non-Abe films are much harder to find unfortunately. (or they are frequently). His non Abe films are much harder to find unfourtently.
label: analysis
notes: Names all four Teshigahara-Abe collaborations (Pitfall, Face of Another, The Man without a Map) with specific quality claims; ranks them ('my second favorite') — filmography as evidence

text: I loved Ex Machina. Went to rewatch it a while back and was really struck by how bad he is in it. I know he’s playing a dork with a pretty grand opinion of himself, but I struggled with his performance. I don’t know how much that is to do with some pretty hammy dialogue, but the other 2 leads are great. I thought I was a fan of his but now I’m struggling to think of a performance I actually liked. Frank was pretty cringe and ‘run’ was unwatchable.
label: reaction
notes: First-person throughout: 'I loved', 'I struggled with his performance', 'I'm struggling to think of a performance I actually liked'; personal re-watch disappointment, no structural argument

text: Killing of a Sacred Deer - I actually forgot this was Yorgos. At first I wasn't vibing and felt like it had almost an old school feeling in a not good way, but as it continued to develop and get weirder, I started really getting into it. It also felt very kubrickian. Ultimately, a four-star movie for me. I watched this over two days because on the first day we suddenly had to put our dog down. So the second day felt very strange finishing out this movie. Ferris Bueller's Day off - It seemed like a great time to rewatch this classic. I recognized some similar dialogue and tropes from other John Hughes films. It also made me wonder what kind of upbringing and family relationships he had. I loved a lot of the establishing shots and parallel shots of what was happening in the school while the kids ditched. It made me very nostalgic for Chicago, especially during my childhood. I realized that a lesser film may have focused more on the kids and their narrative with fewer supporting shots. What's the term for that? Not quite establishing shots.
label: reaction
notes: Personal diary mode dominates: dog died mid-watch, nostalgic for Chicago; parallel-shots observation is incidental to the personal viewing context

text: Nightmare on Elm Street franchise - Got the 4K set so been going through them, 4 down, 3 to go. Unfortunately 5 and 6 are the worst ones so I'm not in the rush to get them watched (even with them being first time watches of the Uncut version of 5 and 6 with the ending actually in 3D as originally released). Hoppers - Went in with low expectations and came out very surprised. Legit hilarious, memorable characters, and fun story. Not perfect, the movie really needed the main character to face her anger issues and its a bit heavy handed with its environmental message, but still a really good time. Scream 7 - There are things I like about this entry and things I really don't like. I don't think it's the dumpster fire people are saying it is but I do think it is still the second weakest of the franchise (with Scream 3 still being the worst). The Bride - I have never seen a movie start so bad just immediately and I don't bail on movies even if they're bad but by around the 30 minute point where she's just been revived I just couldn't take it anymore and stopped. Fuck this movie.
label: reaction
notes: Personal watch log across four films with first-person ratings and reactions; 'Fuck this movie' and 'I just couldn't take it anymore' are experiential not analytical

text: Wolf Hall: The Mirror and the Light (2025) **** The Machiavellian Thomas Cromwell returns ten years later deeply embroiled in Henry VIII's political intrigues and marriages, and Mark Rylance is magnificent. The final episode is some finely wrought drama and a great ending to a terrific series. This conclusion to Cromwell's story even improves the stature of the first series and puts them both on the highly-recommended list. Better Man (2024) *** I don't know why he's an ape, but the gimmick works because I wouldn't have watched the movie otherwise. I didn't know anything about Robbie Williams except that he was a British pop star.  Unfortunately, it's the standard rock music biopic we've seen many times before: the rise and fall due to wealth and drugs and the pressures of fame with occasional musical numbers. It's all done well but it's never surprising. You'd hope a movie that dared to cast an ape as its lead would try to be daring in any other way. I guess he just really loved Planet of the Apes. Wild Zero (1999) ** Zombies, spaceships, punk rockers, arms-dealing yakuza, transgender love: This Japanese movie throws together lots of crazy ideas but doesn't display the filmmaking skills to make it all work. At the very least some tighter editing is desperately needed to give it more momentum. Some good music might have helped too. I won't even ask for good acting. Weekend at Bernie's (1989) *** It's a one joke movie, but the script is actually pretty tight and the lead actors are fun so I have no complaints. The Young Philadelphians (1959) **** Paul Newman's a young lawyer who was born and raised to climb the social ladder only to find it may lead to wealth and status but not happiness. It's a solid drama that suffers from being an adaptation of a popular novel, meaning it runs too long trying to encompass a large timespan with new plot threads introduced late in the story, so it loses momentum. Thankfully a few performances kickstart some life into it. Robert Vaughn is terrific as the unsuccessful version of Newman's character, giving a showy Oscar-nominated performance. Barbara Rush is excellent too as the major rung on his ladder. Most of all, Billie Burke (Glenda, the good witch) shows up late in the film as a quirky elderly philanthropist and steals the movie. Newman is solid and maturing as an actor, and you could follow this with The Verdict to see what the character might end up 20 years later. Merrily We Live (1938) **** A delightfully zany rich family takes in a "tramp" as a chauffeur. The threadbare story follows close on the heels of My Man Godfrey, but it's full of sharp one-liners and the ensemble is excellent despite being lesser known talents. I watched it because Billie Burke won an Oscar and she's hilarious as the matriarch. Vaudeville veteran Clarence Kolb plays a cantankerous father figure doing physical comedy you rarely see in older actors. Alan Mowbray's butler upstages everyone (he was also in My Man Godfrey). Stars Constance Bennett and Brian Aherne are simply charming, with Aherne reminding me a lot of Errol Flynn. Ann Dvorak even pops in as a man-hungry friend. It's a stellar cast just making this as fun as possible. It lulls a bit near the end when it tries to make a story happen, but don't let the story spoil your fun.
label: analysis
notes: Young Philadelphians: specific structural critique (late plot threads kill momentum) + named Oscar-nominated performances (Vaughn, Burke); cross-film comparison to The Verdict; Merrily We Live ensemble analysis

text: Watching Zodiac for the first time. Very Finchery and great story. I just wish he didn't pump a bunch of rock songs in like it was a music video; especially considering it's a film about a serial killer.
label: hot_take
notes: Short (38 words); specific criticism of rock song choices vs. serial-killer subject but undeveloped — one-line tonal observation, no structural argument

text: I don't really get what the present-day closing scene/epilogue is all about. Up to that point, I was loving the film's atmospheric tour through the sights and sounds of mid-'70s Recife as well as its huge cast of extraordinarily vivid characters; I was loving how exciting and funny it was as well as the acuteness of its sociopolitical commentary (sharks are a pretty fantastic metaphor for authoritarianism, as it happens). But then the film suddenly shifts course and becomes about completely different things, narratively, thematically and stylistically. It's such a huge change, and it happens so abruptly, that it completely threw me off-balance to no clear purpose. Anyone else have the same issue?
label: analysis
notes: Identifies present-day epilogue as jarring structural shift; names specific setting (mid-'70s Recife); 'sharks as metaphor for authoritarianism' is specific thematic claim; all falsifiable

text: I wasn't too thrilled by this movie. In a lot of ways it felt incredibly dated. News, how information gets disseminated, funny sounding agencies keeping aliens under wraps, the Aliens themselves etc. I really want to see a movie that deep dives into how it's impossible to really know anything these days, where everyone believes something entirely different and that if disclosure were to occur there would likely be no semblance of a mono-reaction or singular vector by which society finds out. It would be some incredibly messy controversial misunderstanding of all kinds of strange facts and everyone getting their news and ideas off social media. I want to see that side mixed in with a more interesting Alien / contact scenario like Arrival. There's still some Spielberg magic here but this didn't feel like a fully realized movie that captures your imagination like his other films.
label: reaction
notes: Personal disappointment + wish-list for a different film; 'I really want to see a movie that...' is first-person desire not film argument; Arrival cited as positive comparator but not analyzed

text: Been on a Michael Haneke run. Cache (Hidden) this week - the anxiety builds from scene one and never really lets up. Then Funny Games, which is a completely different kind of uncomfortable. Both leave you sitting with the discomfort long after. Anyone else going through a director's whole filmography lately?
label: reaction
notes: 'The anxiety builds from scene one and never really lets up', 'sitting with the discomfort long after' — experiential responses to Haneke; 'different kind of uncomfortable' is comparative but stays in personal register

text: No Hard Feelings (2023, Gene Stupnitsky) Not typically one for rom coms, I enjoyed this one! Jennifer Lawrence is great as Maddie, down on her luck and hired to date an awkward 19 year old about to leave for college. It’s about what you’d expect, but a fun watch. 6/10 Bedazzled (2000, Harold Ramis) This one didn’t age well at all! Brendan Fraser plays Elliot Richards, who is granted seven wishes by the Devil, Elizabeth Hurley. Relies heavily on outdated stereotypes and tropes, had me rolling my eyes throughout. 4/10 Eternity (2025, David Freyne) Mixed feelings about this one. I like media that explores the concept of an afterlife, but this one was essentially two men in a competition over who could win eternity with Elizabeth Olsen. It had some touching deeper moments but was pretty surface level for most of it. 6.5/10
label: reaction
notes: Personal watch log with numeric ratings; first-person preference statements ('Not typically one for rom coms'); critiques are brief and unsupported

text: This week I've picked back up my project to watch and review every Kurosawa film in order, with I Live in Fear and Throne of Blood (which I graded a B- and A respectively). This was my first watch of I Live in Fear (one of the few Kurosawa movies I hadn't seen previously). A few days ago I watched The Backrooms, which I felt was a great concept executed competently, in that the pieces fit together and basically worked, but it never felt like it had the ambition the concept warrants or the creative passion to really delve into new narrative spaces, instead using an interesting setting for very familiar story beats. Of the three big horror releases the last few weeks, it seems the most even-keel, lacking Obsession's drive to push into uncomfortable territory and Passenger's creative passion for shot and scene composition, but also not falling into the excesses of either of them. I think overall I liked it less than Obsession but more than Passenger. I'm trying to make time today for Pressure.
label: analysis
notes: Compares three recent horror films on specific qualities: Backrooms lacks ambition/creative passion; Obsession has drive to push into uncomfortable territory; Passenger has shot/scene composition passion — all falsifiable

text: Got hired to write a short film for an actor to intentionally display his acting chops, so I've been doing some research for the project. The short film I'm working on is an action/drama to be about seven minutes in length, yet tell a full, emotionally impactful story that might lead someone to say "Wow, I didn't realize you could fit so much story in such a short period of time." The films I've watched for research were: Warrior (2011) -First time watching this and loved it, though I couldn't help but root for a separate character than the film itself was rooting for. Run Lola Run (1998) -Awesome late 90s German crime thriller that never hits the brakes once it slams on the gas. It's been on my watchlist for years but never actually took the time to see it until now. Great execution and maintaining the film's intensity even when Lola isn't in motion, and she's almost always in motion. Ben-Hurr (1959) -Another film that's been on my watchlist forever. Excellent cinematography and budding tension in action sequences. I also really enjoyed this experience due to the sheer scale of it -- in many scenes there are loads of people present, real actual people who aren't green screen projections, which is something I feel is lacking in a significant number of films today. Sure, it's likely due to budgets or story purposes, but it was refreshing to see a film that felt truly epic. The Raid: Redemption (2011) The Raid is an old favorite I've seen many-a-time, but it was the first time I watched it for business instead of pleasure. I was studying the overall pacing of the film and how it's an action movie that borrows elements from the horror/slasher genre once the police start being hunted by the criminals. 28 Weeks Later (2007) -I rewatched this primarily for the intro, which showcases a peaceful safehouse being torn apart by a chaotic zombie horde in about seven minutes flat, perfect material for the film I'm working on. Even the rest of the movie holds up pretty well imo, it's no 28 Days Later but I feel it easily earns its place in the series. Separately from my research for the short, I also watched both Backrooms and Obsession in theaters, and wow, what treats they were. While I felt Backrooms was lacking in following through on the mystery it presents, I felt the atmosphere and cinematography were both pretty strong and kept me engaged even when the creatures didn't. Obsession on the other hand might be my favorite horror film since 2023's Late Night with the Devil -- it was fantastic fun watching Inde Navarrette's performance as her character's possession continued to deepen over the runtime. Her performance carried the movie and made me love it a lot better than Backrooms.
label: analysis
notes: The Raid: specific genre-hybridity analysis (action borrowing from slasher once police are hunted); Run Lola Run: pacing observation; 28 Weeks Later: scene timing (7 mins); Obsession: named performance analysis

text: Just finished watching Backrooms. Of course the movie falls short in certain moments (heard a lot of comments about being dragged a lot) but in my humble opinion is brilliant. I believe we may look back at it in some years as a film that truly encapsulated the depression and loneliness that the lockdown brought into our society and specifically the youth. I am not familiar at all with the creepypasta but i could relate a lot to how eerie certain moments were. The description of how the backrooms worked sounded a lot to how people felt their world was while in those formative years, closed spaces were the whole world and the outside was almost plastic and surreal. Truly brilliant on its own way.
label: analysis
notes: Specific thematic claim: Backrooms 'encapsulated the depression and loneliness that the lockdown brought'; backrooms concept as metaphor for lockdown-era experience of closed spaces — film-specific and falsifiable

text: he Mandalorian and Grogu (2026, Jon Favreau) — Probably the most forgettable Star Wars movie to date. Nothing about it is truly terrible, but almost everything is just a little bit subpar. Feels more like an episode of the show that was padded to feature length than a movie. 4/10 The Cook, The Thief, His Wife and Her Lover (1989, Peter Greenaway) — Felt like this was mostly carried by a strong central performance. I’m aware that the fact it’s so unpleasant to watch is intentional, but that doesn’t mean I have to like it, nor that I see any real point to its being such. 5/10 The Hunchback of Notre Dame (1996, Gary Trousdale/Kirk Wise) — Nowhere near the best movie of the Disney Renaissance, but maybe the most fascinating. It’s not exactly breaking new ground to say that the main reason it stands out as such is the character of Frollo, perhaps the only villain in the Disney canon whom the movie actually attempts to turn into a plausible human individual, rather than being left as purely a mythic incarnation of evil. But what really intrigues me is just how little there is to make this gel with the rest of the characters in the movie, who are otherwise mostly the same broad archetypes that you find in the rest of Disney’s output from this era. I guess I could complain about it being tonally inconsistent, and I did have some problems with that, but, on the whole, I think I might actually enjoy it more because of this. Whether intentionally or not, it gives a sense that the evils of our world are attempting to break into the fairy tale setting that we’re used to, which I find to be, if not necessarily better, then at least more interesting than if the whole thing was dark and realistic throughout. Recommended. 7/10 King Kong (1933, Merian C. Cooper/Ernest B. Schoedsack) — re-watch — Don’t have much to add to the last time I talked about this. Still quite possibly the greatest monster movie of all time. 10/10 The Umbrellas of Cherbourg (1964, Jacques Demy) — re-watch — Huh. After finding that I liked this movie a lot more on second viewing than I did on my first, I honestly thought that a third viewing would bump it up to a perfect rating, but, if anything, my opinion of it actually went down slightly. Honestly, despite my praise of it in my previous review, I think I’m still not really sold on the whole concept of doing the movie as a single extended musical number. I don’t mind constant singing in stories like Les Miserables or Hamilton, where the entire story is basically one long succession of massive, operatic emotions, but when Demy has characters breaking into song even for something as mundane as saying they like movies better than opera, it does start to feel like more of a gimmick than a meaningful artistic choice. Nevertheless, this is still an amazing story, shot gorgeously, and despite my reservations about the basic concept, the movie-long-number itself is quite a remarkable feat of composition. So I’m still keeping this as a must-watch. 9/10 Movie of the week: King Kong
label: analysis
notes: Frollo identified as 'only Disney villain turned into a plausible human individual' — specific comparative claim across Disney canon; Umbrellas: musical-number analysis with Les Miserables/Hamilton comparison; Mandalorian: padded-episode structural critique

text: Moloch (2022, Nico van den Brink) Another Shudder film, but was pleasantly surprised. I like folksy type horror and this one set in the Netherlands was a good watch. 6/10 Backrooms (2026, Kane Parsons) Surprised by all of the hype this one is getting, breaking A24’s box office record for opening weekend! I really liked this one, they nailed the unsettling eerie feeling perfectly. Never heard of the term “liminal spaces” before, now it’s all I’m seeing. 7.5/10 Civil War (2024, Alex Garland) Really like Alex Garland, this one has been on my watchlist for a while now. It was a pretty good watch, but I found myself feeling irritated by the character Jessie more than anything. 6.5/10 The Good Son (1993, Joseph Ruben) Macaulay Culkin and Elijah Wood as children, playing the good and bad son. Macaulay was great at portraying Henry, the sociopath dangerous kid. Unsettling watch. 6/10
label: reaction
notes: Personal watch log with ratings (6/10, 7.5/10, 6.5/10, 6/10); 'I found myself feeling irritated by the character Jessie' is personal character reaction; quality claims are brief

text: Castles for Two (1917, dir. Frank Reicher) A charming romantic comedy. Castles for Two is the story of a stubborn landlord bleeding his tenants dry and the wealthy American subverting him at every turn by supplying the tenants with all the money they need. The film plays around with the characters' identities in creative and fun ways, effectively allowing the American to transform the landlord's character into that of a more conscientious man. The dynamic is complex enough to bypass any questions about the ethics of lying for romantic leverage, and the tone is very light and pleasant. The archetypal lines in which the characters are drawn are more evocative of European fairy tails than traditional dramatic cinema, something that's amplified by the presence of fairies bringing the characters together. Overall, it's not a groundbreaking film, but it is very pleasant, and I think it holds up as a film today. The Clodhopper (1917, dir. Victor Schertzinger) The Clodhopper comes close to making a profound, interesting statement, but it ultimately falls short. The Clodhopper is the story of Everett Nelson, a young, ambitious man who's shunned by his agrarian father and makes his way to New York, where he pulls down a handsome salary dancing on Broadway in a show meant to emulate the typical rural American countryside. There's a cynical edge to The Clodhopper that I find interesting. Everett lands this job not because he auditioned for a bunch of roles across the city but because a director happened to see him and thought, pretty much at a glance, that he had the right stuff. Although Everett does come from a rural background, the show feels inauthentic, a self-referential bit of commentary about how the poor are portrayed in this practically brand-new medium we call cinema. Real poor people live lives in mediocrity, never getting the spotlight, but Everett can pull down ten thousand a week pretending to be one. In the climax, he uses his enormous profits to pay out the panicked clients of his father's rural bank, therefore preventing a minor economic collapse that reminds me, with my 110 years of hindsight, of Black Monday twelve years later. The people of Everett's old town survive off of what Everett can earn at their expense, but that cannot last. Most, if not all, of this subtext is purely unintentional. I know this because Schertzinger likely didn't know that the American banking system was going to explode twelve years hence, and also because Everett is not treated by the text as a selfish, vain performer. He's a wide-eyed dreamer with ambitions so much bigger than his one-horse town. The problem here is that Everett is not likeable or interesting. We're not given a reason to root for him. All he has is some vague dream about not working on his father's farm, so when he slacks off, he just comes off like an entitled little prick. The father is no saint, but we don't get enough of his flaws to justify Everett's behavior. What we're left with is a kid with his head in the clouds navigating a story with no self-awareness. As interesting as The Clodhopper can seem, it's really only skin-deep at the end of the day. Friday the 13th (1980, dir. Sean S. Cunningham) My favorite YouTube channel is Red Letter Media, and I really love Best of the Worst. When I was watching Friday the 13th, I could see in it a lot of the bad slashers they cover on BOTW, and it was enough to make me wonder just why and how Friday the 13th became a cultural phenomenon. It isn't exactly original and daring; Halloween and The Texas Chainsaw Massacre had come out many years earlier, and both were technically so much better. The scares aren't very effective, and the gore really betrays its low budget. The writing is sophomoric, and the acting isn't much better. I expected this from an '80s slasher, but I did hope there would be a little more. But there isn't. Narratively, they really nailed down the formula for this genre. The best I can say about Friday the 13th is that I really liked the lazy summer atmosphere and what little we saw of the small town with its paranoia and Yankee eccentricities. All of that is original, and hard to replicate in a million-dollar horror franchise designed to make people scream as loud as they can. Bugonia (2025, dir. Yorgos Lanthimos) Bugonia is a remake of a much older Korean comedy movie called Save the Green Planet!, something I didn't learn about until after I finished the film. I thought that this made Bugonia make a lot more sense. The idea that the ultra-wealthy are aliens parading as humans is an old one, or at least one that feels like it came from a more innocent time when half of the internet posted memes of Barack Obama with lizard eyes and the other glazed Elon Musk. Bugonia takes the idea that Emma Stone is an alien pretty seriously, which, because of the dated nature of the theory, even among conspiracy theorists, felt jarring and a little contrived. But the exact theory matters less than what it says about the man posing it and the woman countering it. The characters in Bugonia are complex, wavering between sadistic and sympathetic between and even within scenes. Jesse Plemons portrays irrational desperation in a particularly compelling way, and I like the evolution of Stone's interactions with Plemons and his brother. She's such a good manipulator she might even convince you that she's bought into the brothers' bullshit or that she intends anything less than violent retribution once this is all over. Because of this film's still recent release, I don't want to go into the ending, but I would have preferred it to be more ambiguous. But this doesn't really damage the film overall. It's still very good - tense, violent, well-paced, and just a little funny. Resident Evil: Death Island (2023, dir. Eiichiro Hasumi) The director of Resident Evil: Vendetta, the best Resident Evil movie, returns for another explosion of schlock and stupidity. This is probably the most condensed RE movie, featuring only two setpieces and a pretty laser-focused plot. Hasumi understands that you only watch RE movies for dumb action, and that's what he gives you. Don't look for a good script here. It's less bonkers than Vendetta, but I can't act like I'm not having a good time here. Lake Mungo (2008, dir. Joel Anderson) Chilling and moving. Lake Mungo has a very no frills storyline that seems perfectly suited for a documentary, where things can emerge into the plot without feeling like they were made up on the fly, but make no mistake: it's very effective at what it sets out to do. The exploration of grief is gripping, and ties into the horror aspects in very satisfying ways. Just as Alice Palmer's life and death raise more questions than they could ever hope to answer, the Palmer family's haunting shakes the characters' emotional and intellectual understanding of the world and their place within it. This is what all ghost stories should be, stories about literal ghosts and figurative ones, the shadows the lost leave on their home or the heart of a loved one. All of this culminates in a scene so illogical, so surreal, so frightening that it makes you want to turn all the lights on and wait for the sun to rise. No questions are really answered in the film, but the human core is coherent and compelling enough to tide you over. After all, in the face of true grief, what other choice do we have?
label: analysis
notes: The Clodhopper: self-referential social commentary about cinema's portrayal of the poor; FT13 vs. Halloween/TCM: specific comparators with technique claims; Lake Mungo: 'ghost stories about literal and figurative ghosts' thematic interpretation

text: X-Men: The Last Stand (2006, dir. Brett Ratner) Every Empire Strikes Back needs its Return of the Jedi, I guess. I wasn't terribly invested in the X-Men trilogy, even after X2 pleasantly lived up to its positive reputation, but this film was pretty rough. As a conclusion to the series, it's not very satisfying, throwing together Jean Grey's resurrection with an ideological tipping point between various mutant factions and the US government that doesn't actually resolve anything. The metaphor for mutations - race, religion, sexual orientation, what have you - falls apart when the idea of a cure is brought up. A cure is easy to understand for in-universe reasons, but what exactly does it represent in a broader meta argument? The film can't make up its mind, and ignores any finer subtext in favor of cool action scenes and overzealous special effects. The ultimate conclusion is that, no matter what, a mutant should choose to express and embrace themselves however they see fit, which is something that a) marginalized groups whom mutants represent already understand, and b) is not resolved on a systemic level. Can a mutant one day be elected president? Will anti-mutant prejudice actually be curbed on all levels of society? I don't know. I think the film wants me to think so, because the president is a mutant sympathizer, but the government still weaponized the cure, and exploited a mutant to get it. More work than a battle on Alcatraz needs to be done. How does Jean fit into all this? She doesn't. She's a pawn characters vie and fight for, with no ideological stakes or real motivation. The concept of the Phoenix seems like it was invented by a guy in the 1960s who saw a woman in a tube top and developed a fear of female sexuality. Phoenix, lacking all inhibitions, wants to fuck guys until they disintegrate, and is so out of control that only Logan can stop her - with murder. It's crass and grossly unsatisfying, which I guess is consistent for this movie. Characters lack coherent motivations, have thin personalities, and lack strong dynamics. Characters like Scott and Professor X die for convenience's sake, and don't really feel earned or dramatically satisfying. Logan's last line is a joke about a character he barely knows, not a reassuring word shared with Rogue or Storm. Just because Bryan Singer was away doing Superman shit, that doesn't excuse lousy writing. The Kid Brother (1927, dir. Harold Lloyd) A cute, kinetic comedy. Harold Lloyd is excellent before and behind the camera, orchestrating delightful bits of slapstick and energetic chase scenes. Lloyd as Harry Hickory, the dim son of a county sheriff, is charming in a dopey way, and it never feels like the movie clowns on him too hard. He's given the opportunity to act on behalf of the community, and boy does he take it. There isn't too much to talk about in regards to the story, but that's the last thing you're thinking about as Lloyd slinks around a sinking steamship. Once Upon a Time in America (1984, dir. Sergio Leone) An incredible piece of cinema. Through three often intertwining timeframes, Leone explores the evolution of American organized crime from cliques governing ethnic boroughs in New York to doubletalking executives on the fringes of a flattening society. The story of Once Upon a Time in America is a complex one, touching on themes like the loss of innocence, the American dream, the melting pot, remorse, nostalgia, and ambition, among others. While OUATIA is hard to succinctly summarize, all of these ideas crystalize nicely and coherently, which within a daunting 220-minute runtime is a monumental achievement. It will take a full afternoon to watch OUATIA, but you'll never be bored or looking at the clock. If you won't be ensnared by the moral ambivalence and emotional ambiguity, you'll definitely be hooked by the filmmaking. Leone takes the tricks that served him well in the Dollars Trilogy - brilliant compositions, command of silence, liberal use of close-ups - and dials them up to eleven. OUATIA is at once epic and intimate, detailed and broad, able to mine equal drama out of five decades of degradation and five minutes of tense violence. If I were to identify a flaw in OUATIA, it's in its treatment of female characters. The film is unrelenting in its depiction of gangsters and doesn't moralize. Rape occurs in the film, and it's brutal and horrible. I appreciate the intentions behind each rape scene, not to glorify the crime but to demonstrate just how far these assaulting characters have fallen, but problems arise when it comes to the victims' characterizations. The victims are given much less time to develop than their assaulters, and their reactions come off more as jilted lovers than someone who's had their body violated in a traumatic, violent way. As a result, these scenes are trivialized. Leone places more importance on Robert de Niro or James Woods' emotional turmoil than their victims'. While this gives the film a nasty bruise, OUATIA is still 220 minutes, and Debra and Carol are still well-written characters. At that length, it would be hard not to have at least something going for you. I don't know if OUATIA redeems itself, but I also don't know if it needs to. The film is practically a world unto itself, and the fact that it makes sense and packs such an emotional punch matters more than almost anything. Oculus (2013, dir. Mike Flanagan) After the awkward Absentia, Flanagan finds his niche and snuggles up. Oculus is reminiscent of many of Flanagan's later works, namely The Haunting of Hill House, and while it isn't as good as that miniseries, it's not too far off the mark. Trauma and the supernatural are snugly tied together in this film, resulting in compelling character dynamics in the first half and nightmarish surrealism in the second. Karen Gillan and Brenton Thwaites are great, the editing is tight, and the story never loses its humanity even as things get scarier and scarier. This film set the tone for the rest of Flanagan's career, and I'm elated that it did. The Hit (1984, dir. Stephen Frears) A great tourist ad for Spain. Frears finds the most visually-striking locales in Spain, all the way from the arid interior to the forests of the Basque Country, and shoots them in the most visually-striking ways he can. Paired with a mellow soundtrack on, fittingly, a Spanish guitar, the direction is captivating enough to keep the viewer's attention. A lot of this, though, is due to a fairly flat story. John Hurt and Tim Roth abduct Terence Stamp on behalf of a mob boss, whom Stamp had testified against ten years ago, who intends to personally kill Stamp. For much of the film, the characters appear one-dimensional, with Hurt leaning into his tough guy persona, Roth a bit more convivial, and Stamp like a plucky tourist. There's little to attach yourself to until the third act, when Stamp lays out his ethos - he's been expecting this reprisal for so long that he feels a sense of relief, even though he knows he's going to die. It's an interesting idea for a character, but it doesn't recontextualize much of the preceding film in an interesting way, and he doesn't have enough time to really explore it before the film ends. Hurt, with his contradictory actions, gets no revelation of his own, and ends the film one-dimensional. The plot is very thin, and the only thing keeping the film up is its style, which can't patch over the rougher parts of the script, but can entertain. Before I Wake (2016, dir. Mike Flanagan) Another great outing by Flanagan. Before I Wake continues the narrative trends of Oculus, exploring the intersection of trauma and the supernatural, but tells its story in a dark fantasy context. It's like Ursula K. Le Guin's The Lathe of Heaven mixed with The Babadook. Before I Wake tells the story of Jessie, a mother who's lost her child, taking in a foster child who, as it turns out, can manifest his dreams into reality. She abuses this ability to interact with a facsimile of her deceased son, creating friction with her husband and dabbling with powers she doesn't understand. The concept behind Jessie as a character is pretty fucked up, but the film tells her her story with such emotional honesty that it's hard not to sympathize with her. We're not asked to look the other way, but only to try to understand how a parent can struggle with such a traumatic loss, how moving on is not a linear process and can hurt people if not done healthily. Through this, Flanagan creates some really spiffing sequences that are as visually interesting as they are scary. A lot of this is hurt, unfortunately, by a Psycho-like exposition dump that tries and largely fails to take the film down a sentimentalist route and undermines a lot of the metaphorical power of the film's supernatural content. You don't need to explain everything, Mike. Fortunately, a lot of his content after Before I Wake is a lot more balanced in this regard, and it is generally nice to move on from the pessimism of Absentia and Oculus. Ouija (2014, dir. Stiles White) Maybe I spoke too soon calling Friday the 13th a generic slasher. If you directed this script on a shoestring budget in 1984, it wouldn't have even won most BOTWs. Ouija is a nothing burger of a horror movie that isn't scary, isn't emotionally compelling, isn't funny, isn't anything. The only thing that's noteworthy about it is that Mike Flanagan worked on its prequel, which is apparently very good. That's the whole reason I even watched this piece of shit. I know nothing about Origin of Evil, but I have a feeling that getting all this vital context wasn't worth it.
label: analysis
notes: X-Men: mutation-metaphor critique with specific ideological analysis (cure = ???); OUATIA: Leone technique compared to Dollars Trilogy (compositions, silence, close-ups); Before I Wake: Le Guin/Babadook comparisons; Oculus vs. Haunting of Hill House

text: The Natural (1984, Barry Levinson) — The Natural did not feel particularly cliched to me, which was a pleasant surprise, since I usually expect that to be my biggest complaint about inspirational sports dramas like this. On the other hand, I’m not exactly sure what it did feel like. I’m tempted to complain about how silly and mawkish it still feels, but it goes so far into it that I’m not entirely sure whether to evaluate it as a straight mawkish drama or as a subtle-but-deliberate parody of one. I guess I’ll just leave this as Not sure how to rate. Magnolia (1999, Paul Thomas Anderson) — This was very different than I was expecting. Based on its reputation, I thought it’d be a classic hyperlink narrative, where a bunch of seemingly-unrelated storylines gradually converge into one. But that’s not really the case; while several connections between the characters do get revealed, the storylines still remain largely separate, with what convergence exists being more emotional and thematic than literal. It’s actually a remarkable achievement that Anderson manages to keep the entire thing feeling like a single, cohesive, and tightly-written narrative, given how little explicit connection there is between all its different threads. Pretty close to a perfect movie. 10/10 Munich (2005, Steven Spielberg) — I'm not sure how to talk substantially about this movie without getting into things that are more radioactive than I’d like to talk about here. So, for now, all I’ll say is that this is a much darker and more complex work than I’d have expected from Spielberg, and he handles it beautifully. One of his best works, which is saying a lot. 10/10 Movie of the week: Magnolia
label: analysis
notes: Magnolia: identifies mismatch between hyperlink-narrative expectation and actual structure (emotional/thematic convergence not literal) — specific structural observation; The Natural: parody-vs-straight-drama interpretive question

text: A lot of Chanbara/Jidaigeki. I've come out of it feeling that Kurosawa is a perfectly competent director, but is a little overhyped in my opinion. Favorites from this genre: Harakiri - Tense, twisting, and with some incredible payoffs. The decay of samurai as a class is a common theme in these films, and I feel that Harakiri is where it reaches its zenith. (Questions of the historicity of the honorable or ideal samurai aside) At this stage in my life, I feel like movies that really hit this emotional register for me are few and far between. Go see this if you haven't already. Kagemusha - An epic meditation on the illusion of power. Hitokiri/Tenchu - A touching deconstruction of the samurai genre. Izo is a thug with delusions of grandeur; a loyal, fearless fighter who can't comprehend anything beyond the tip of his sword.
label: analysis
notes: Names Harakiri, Kagemusha, Hitokiri/Tenchu with specific thematic claims per film; 'Harakiri is where [samurai class decay] reaches its zenith' is falsifiable against the genre

text: I let time pass after its release and actually forgot it was even made, even though I had been looking forward to it. A recent interview of her called it back to mind. I thought it was going to be a small, almost quaint, artistic film that had a personal aura about it. I was not prepared for the power of the emotion, the towering presence of a depressed, removed, pencil-cracking, angry main character (performance), and especially not ready for how absolutely inspired it was in terms of filmmaking itself. A Proustian, Sound-and-the-Fury, intensive exploration of memory and trauma. Usually I resist all sorts of flashback motifs and editing techniques, but this film completely invaded me with both emotion (as if it were my own memories) and sensation. Again and again I pulled into the familiar of what I have sensed in life, in the privacy of my own being. And the treatment of abuse and its after image...it dove so deep down into it. The closest thing I could think of was Aftersun (2022) which approached suicide and depression from an oblique angle of childhood and sensation. This began in that realm, but sent me so far into the maze of what makes a person, I was pretty taken aback that Kristen Stewart could make such a film. I think it must be watched on a big screen in a dark room, right up against all that sensation and affect. You have to be swallowed by it to really get what it is doing. You have to be drowning in it, jolted by the cuts, just as she was. Felt a little bit like getting into a theatrical car accident, in how it reverberates in me far beneath the cerebral, and even emotional layers. Very impressed upon.
label: reaction
notes: 298 words of first-person sensation: 'completely invaded me', 'as if it were my own memories'; Aftersun comparison used to describe personal experience not make an analytical argument; drowning/car-accident metaphors

text: Cairo Station directed by Youssef Chahine (Egypt/1958) -  This is a melodrama clearly inspired by Italian Neo-realism.  Set entirely in and around the central train station in Cairo and the motley group of poor laborers and snack vendors who dodge the railroad authorities and argue and flirt with one another day in and day out.  The film’s protagonist is a sad, thin, limping newspaper hawker named Kinawi (played by the film’s director).  He is smitten with the loud and brassy drink vendor Hannuma, who tolerates him, but she is eager to marry a macho baggage handler named Abu Siri (who is meanwhile trying to form a union).   The heat, the noise, and the filthiness of the environment doesn’t bring out the best in any of these people (an exception is the pious muslim named Madbouli, who works a newspaper stand and hires poor Kinawi, even finding him an abandoned railroad shack to sleep in). It’s a watchable film, notable for its sophisticated cinematography, but some contemporary viewers may find it all a bit frantic and exaggerated.  I’m told that Egyptian audiences at the time of its theatrical release were unimpressed and perhaps a bit put off by its grim tone and story. The Music Room directed by Satyajit Ray (India/1958) —  This is a good film that might have been a great film.  Set in the 1920s, it is the story of a once wealthy landowner, his wife, and teenage son living in their crumbling ancestral estate on ever diminishing funds.  The protagonist is Biswambhar Roy, who has neglected his duties as a landowner, but has a genuine and passionate love for hosting musical evenings in his manor’s elegant music room.  It is not just the delight of the live performances that he finds so satisfying - he is also anxious to host his peers (all male) and retain familial pride through their appreciation and esteem.  When a family tragedy occurs, Biswambhar closes off the music room and refuses all invitations and social engagements for four years.  Eventually a visit from a disliked nouveau riche neighbor prompts him, out of spite, to reopen the room for one last performance by local musicians and a famed female dancer.  He invites his friends for the final time (he literally pays the musicians with the last money he has).  The performance is wonderful and the dancer is indeed mesmerizing.  But upon its conclusion Biswambhar has nothing left (except for two loyal servants and a fine horse he could never bring himself to part with). This is a beautiful and moving film.  Unfortunately, it is paced so slowly that at times it feels amateurish (the first two of three musical performances are grating and interminable).  Also, the third performance with the dancer is terrific, but implausible, since the dancer is clearly not singing, despite our hearing a female singer throughout the performance (the small group of accompanying musicians are all male).  Lastly, symbolism is sometimes heavy-handed (example: wind from a strong storm blows through an open window and topples a small decorative ship on a shelf, which the protagonist sets upright - this same storm is the cause of the family crisis).  The revered actor Chhabi Biswas plays the tragic protagonist and he is truly great in this film (he’s in practically every scene). In Bruges directed by Martin McDonagh (UK/2008) - This first film by an admired playwright is very funny and very violent.  The plot is simple: a novice hitman (Colin Farrell) botches his first job and the crime boss who hired him puts him under the watchful eye of a more experienced killer (Brendon Gleeson) and sends both to Belgium to lie low.  The older man is genuinely fond of his whiney and remorseful charge and delights in this impromptu vacation in the charming medieval town of Bruges. The younger man hates the place and passes the time drinking beer, chasing a local girl, becomes obsessed with an American dwarf actor in town for a movie shoot, and can’t wait for their boss to allow them to return to London.  Nothing goes well for any of these three, rather likable, criminals.  Great dialogue.  Two fine performances by two skilled Irish actors.  Great scenery.  See it.
label: analysis
notes: The Music Room: specific structural critique (first two of three performances grating), technical flaw (dancer not singing), specific symbolic scene cited (ship toppled by wind) — all falsifiable
